All right, so in this video, we're gonna be talking about market sophistication. Now I want to be 100% clear that I'm going to try to make this as simple and as easy as possible to understand for you, because I know that a lot of people when they first understand or they first hear market sophistication, they're like, what is this? But this is essentially going to help you understand why e-commerce is so hard or why creating winning ads is really hard. Like if you don't understand this is extremely hard to compete in sophisticated markets. Okay. And if you've ever heard the word saturated, that's essentially what market sophistication is, but we're going to break it down today. I'm going to make it very easy and very simple for you to understand. And ultimately again, remember that we're understanding these things piece by piece, product desire, market sophistication, we're going to understand avatars, we're going to understand angles. And then again, I'm going to put all of this together, but let's go ahead and jump at the screen to get started with helping you clearly understand. And even if you've seen the other training on market sophistication, you should 100% watch this because it is going to make it so much easier for you to process and know how to actually apply this to your ads or better yet, how to actually just be aware of it when writing your ads. All right. So as you see here, why you need to understand market sophistication before creating an avatar. Well, again, if you don't know, this is Eugene Schwartz, you know, shocker. It's his theory. And he actually created this theory as you can see here in 1966, and it is actually still relevant today. That's what I love so much about marketing is if you actually get to the fundamentals and to the core of marketing, a lot of this stuff still applies. Right. You have the product, you have the desires of what people want. You have the sophistication levels. You have the specific people that you're going after. You have the angle, essentially how you're going after them. Right. So there, all of this stuff still applies. It's just the vehicle in which we apply it. Maybe back 50 years ago, it was newspapers or it was, you know, magazines or it was direct mail where now it's on social media. Okay. But it still applies. Okay. All right. Category four, behaviors and habits. We are on the home stretch. Behaviors are regular action patterns that people develop as responses to their experience, emotions, desires, or demographics in simple terms. Behaviors are how people act. Okay. They are the things that we do and oftentimes are predictable. Okay. My behavior is to work a lot. You can assume probably nine with 90% accuracy. It's like, what's Spence doing right now? Probably working. Okay. But behaviors are often very specific solutions to problems from experiences. Okay. And so a lot of experiences happen, right? So you tried X and Y happened, or just something occurred to you. And now as a result, you do Z instead, or you do something as instead. Okay. So that's how behaviors can develop from experiences. Avoid combining more than one primary desire in a single avatar. Keep that core drive singular. Second, the more categories you layer on top, the more specific and usually the more effective the avatar becomes. And the third rule is maybe the most critical one for anyone trying to scale. Right. The more broad your avatar, so the fewer categories you use, the better your product, your offer, your brand. And frankly, your marketing skill has to be to even compete. So the flip side of that coin is... Conversely, and this is where the power really hits home, the more specific your sub avatar is, the less innovative or groundbreaking your product or your offer needs to be to build that immediate trust and get conversions. Wow. Yeah. Specific messaging really earns you the right to be a little less polished in other areas. Okay, so let's start with category one. Category one must always be desires. This has to be the starting point because desires hold that emotional pull, that fundamental want or need that actually compels someone to make a purchase. If you don't know what they want, you can't sell to them. We hear about those big core desires, right? Like health, wealth, status. Are those too broad to start with? Way too broad. Those are the underlying human drivers for sure. But for initial avatar creation, we need surface level desires. We need the specific immediate outcome they're actually looking for. Can you give us an example of the difference? Sure. So if the core desire is health, the surface desire is I need to lose this specific 10 pounds of baby fat. If the core desire is control, the surface desire is I want my dog to stop pulling on the leash during our evening walks so I don't look bad. Okay, the specificity makes it instantly actionable. And you've got that simple test for listeners, the desire checker. Right. Can you use the sentence, I want X, or I need Y? So if someone is complaining their dental routine takes forever, the desire isn't health. It's I want to brush my teeth faster. If you can say it like that, you've found your starting point. Exactly. So the next layer after desire is experiences. These are situations people have gone through either in the past or right now. And experiences so unifying because they bring people together who have been through the same thing. The sources break these down into two types. The first one is pretty straightforward. situational experiences. Right. Situational experiences are just life events, current circumstances like my bedroom faces east and gets blasted with sunlight at 530 in the morning. Yeah. Or I just had a baby and I am severely sleep deprived. External, shareable situations. And the second type, product based experiences that connects directly back to what the market is already doing. Right, what they've tried. Exactly. Product based experiences speak to what solutions the customer has already tried and importantly failed with. For example, I tried two different brands of blackout curtains and I still woke up tired. Or I spent $80 on a dog harness and my dog still pulls. Yes, that is so powerful because it instantly separates your message from all the beginners. It establishes you as the expert who understands their past failures. It acknowledges their journey. I mean, a generic ad for a new curtain might get ignored. But if you say, tired of wasting money on blackout curtains that still let light bleed through the sides, you've built instant credibility. And this is where we have to stress one key point. For building this core avatar, you must isolate the experience. Just focus on what happened. Don't assume the emotion or the behavior that came from it. Not yet. Let's use that cat example. If our core desire is broad, like wants cat to be healthy, and we layer in the experience cat is excessively licking its paws, leading to bald spots. That combination instantly creates a sub avatar who knows exactly what you're talking about. Now you're getting it. Yes. Category three is emotions. This is how people feel about the desire or the experience. And emotions are arguably the most potent trigger because they often just bypass logical thinking completely. And you mentioned the classic split, six universal primary emotions, fear, joy, sadness versus the secondary emotions, which are more complex, like anxious, frustrated or confident. Yeah. And when you find a secondary emotion, it's a signal to dig a little deeper. If someone feels anxious about their credit card debt, that anxiety is probably rooted in a primary emotion, most likely the fear of financial ruin. So you can use the secondary one in the ad copy, but knowing the primary one helps you craft the core appeal. Exactly. And this is where the real nuance in messaging comes from. Let's take two people with the same desire. One wants a six pack and feels motivated and confident. The messaging to them is all about optimization, speed, getting there faster. Okay. But the other person who wants a six pack, but feels totally defeated or ashamed because they've failed so many times, that person requires a completely different tone. You have to be more supportive, acknowledge the failure without blaming them. You got it. Same with experience. If their dog is pulling and they feel embarrassed because people are watching them struggle, that's one avatar. But if the same dog is pulling and they feel scared that the dog will run into the street, that's a survival based emotion. Two totally distinct avatars defined by the feeling, not just the situation. Next up is behaviors and habits. These are the predictable patterns, the actions people develop in response to their desires, experiences, and emotions. They really reveal how someone approaches their life. This category seems to connect most strongly with a person's ego or their self identity. Absolutely. When you successfully target a behavior, you are targeting who that person believes they are, their identity. If they see themselves as a meal prepper, they will respond to messaging that confirms that identity. So if you understand the behavior, you can almost predict their next move. You can basically tell them the future in your ads, which creates this almost uncanny sense of, wow, they get me. Let's run through some combinations. Take the experience of having kids who threw tantrums in public. The resulting behavior. This parent always brings a huge bag of snacks, water, toys, everything, even for a short trip to the store. So the marketing angle is. If you're the parent who can't leave the house without enough supplies for a three day trek, then this product is for you. Yeah. You've instantly identified their preparation habit. Or a desire wants to lose 20 pounds. The behavior is they already meal prep every single Sunday. Right. So the ad targets them by saying, if you still meal prep every Sunday, but haven't seen the results you want, then X. You're speaking directly to their established habit. And you said mental behaviors count too. For sure. If someone feels insecure about their skin, that's the emotion there. The behavior might be that they constantly order new skincare products, always searching. The ad could say, tired of filling your bathroom cabinet with products you only use twice. So the behavior checker is simple. Does this describe what the person does? Physically or mentally. Overthinking every financial decision counts just as much as wakes up at 5 a.m. to meditate. Exactly. Which brings us finally to demographics, static factual things, age, gender, employment, location. They're the least important for driving the motivation to buy. But they feel necessary sometimes, right? I mean, some products only apply to certain people. They are absolutely necessary for logistical reasons. And a lot of the time they're implied by the other categories anyway. If your avatar's experience is caring for aging parents, you can safely assume an older average age. The crucial error is starting there. So when are they genuinely useful then? They're useful for adding one extra layer of relatability or, like you said, logistical accuracy, especially if the product has a physical limitation. A brand selling running gear might find their winning avatar wants to run a marathon but fears knee injury needs to be filtered by age 40 plus, because the physical risk is just higher there. Let's go back to that great example of the two very different people, the 22 year old woman and the 54 year old man. Yes, they might share the same desire, finding a partner, same experience, using dating sites and getting ghosted, same Dajan, emotion, feeling defeated, same behavior, constantly checking their phone. The only thing separating them is demographics, age and gender. And if you start your targeting with female 20s, you needlessly cut out a huge pool of potential buyers whose underlying psychological pain, the real driver, is identical. Starting with the Core 4, make sure you maximize your market for the pain you saw. OK, that makes perfect sense. But what about beliefs? That seems like a big one. That's a question that always comes up. Our sources confirm that beliefs aren't a separate category because they're actually the result of combining the Core 5. So if you find a belief, you use it as a starting point to kind of reverse engineer the actionable avatar. Exactly. Let's take the belief. I believe I can't lose weight because my genetics are against me. That sounds powerful. But how do you target it? Right. You translate it. You ask what happened to make them believe this. That's the experience, trying and failing diets. How does this belief make them feel? That's the emotion, hopelessness, anger. What do they do because of it? The behavior crash dieting every January. So that translation process gives you four distinct angles for your ads instead of just one general statement about genetics. That's the key to making it actionable. Now, let's talk about scaling this because the number of avatars you should create really depends on where your business is at. OK, so if a listener is just starting out, say, zero to 100k a month, what's the strategy? You have to be more specific to start. You focus intensely on one core avatar, preferably based on a really strong desire, and then you build out tons of sub avatars around it by layering on experiences, emotions, behaviors. You earn the right to go broader later. You have to. Your goal is maximum resonance in a small, deep pool. So if my core is wants to lose 10 pounds, my sub avatars could be wants to lose 10 pounds but feels defeated or wants to lose 10 pounds and meal preps every Sunday. You go deep, not wide. Precisely. Now, once you hit that million plus a month level, the whole scope changes. You need to start expanding to maybe three, five, ten solid core avatars. But this requires world class execution and just tremendous volume. And that's where that example of comfort comes in, generating that staggering amount of content. Yes, they scale by generating over 10,000 pieces of content a week. Why? Because at that level of ad spend, you need content that resonates with every single one of those microscopic sub avatars you've defined. You're trying to maximize relatability at a massive scale. You're making sure that no matter the specific combination of desire, experience, emotion and behavior, there's a piece of creative out there designed to read that specific customer's mind. It's a combination of deepening your psychological understanding and just massively increasing your personalized output. That really feels like the roadmap. It is. Wow. This is fundamentally redefined how I think we should all approach buyer profiles. So to quickly recap the core five, start with desires, layer on the unifying context of experiences, identify that driving force of emotions, look for the resulting predictable behaviors, and then finally use demographics to add logistical accuracy. The secret really is in the combinations. By creating these sub avatars that are so specific, they feel like they are literally reading the customer's mind. You get that powerful relevance and that leads directly to lower costs and conversion rates that your competitors can only dream about. And as we learned, the more specific and insightful your avatar is, the less pressure there is on your product to be truly innovative right out of the gate. So consider this. If you could identify the single most unique ego based behavior avatar in your market, a group that feels completely misunderstood by every other brand, how much worse or less innovative could your product actually be and still dominate the market? That segment simply because your messaging was spot on. That's the true disruptive power of actionable avatars.

12.How To Add Desires Features Benefits -

Evolve · Evolve

All right, so now as we get to filling out the growth guide, this is gonna be the final step to

and attract others is kind of the same as wanting to get attention and have others compliment
their hair. So when you're listing out the desires, just know that it's okay if they're not 100%
accurate, okay? And just basically the whole goal is it's just to get our ideas into the sheet, okay?
So just remember that market desires are I want, and remember that we cannot create desires,
okay? We can only channel them. So when you're thinking of these desires, these are the I want
this, I want that, what are the bigger picture side of things, okay? Then once we get into the
features and benefits, this is a really great exercise to kind of lay everything out.

you don't have to feel like you're getting a workout every time you dry your hair, is there a

can tap into, or maybe this is just something that people don't really care about.

right? So maybe that is something where we come back and we add another desire where it's
like, they just want a high quality drying experience, right? Like they wanna have the ability to dry
their hair without all the hassle of typical hair dryers, right? That just might just be a different

research stuff, okay? Very, very simple, 110,000 RPM motor, microprocessor intelligent chip, tricolor light ring, one pound lightweight build.
And so for example, here's one where I actually split it up by two. And this is something that you
can do with some of these other features that maybe have more benefits, right? 110,000 RPM

right? The matching desire is for people that want to save time by drying their hair faster. So
that's gonna be the main feature that helps them do that, right? And then if we go down to, for
example, the 59 decibels of sound.
So you can dry your hair at any time of the day, early in the morning with roommates or getting
ready for school, okay? They wanna be healthier and have healthier, shiny hair. So is that
something that maybe sound is a whole new desire to be able to dry? They want to be able to dry
their hair at any time of day. That could be a desire, but is that a massive desire? Probably not as

this with our desires to make sure that we understand most importantly this, right? Do these
features contribute to the desire that people actually want? And what are the benefits of that,

13.How To Add Awareness Levels -

Evolve · Evolve

Alright, so this next tab in here is going to be talking about awareness, and this is something that
is honestly extremely, extremely helpful to better understand, kind of when you're reviewing ads,
but also at the same time, trying to figure out what angles you should be focusing on and what
kind of questions you need to be answering. So, for example, if I was selling an air purifier, I'm
now going to go down the list of the five stages of market awareness and what questions my
ideal customer is probably asking themselves, right? So if we start with most aware, we're
probably looking at, for example, if I'm in the market for an air purifier and I'm most aware, I'm
probably thinking, what's the least expensive air purifier that does the best job, right? Like right
now, I already know I need one, I already know I want one, I'm just trying to figure out, alright,
what's the best offer that I can get, right? That's a very simple question. The second, that's going
to be pretty much the same for almost all products.
The next question here is product aware, right? So again, we're talking about air purifier here.
How much space can an air purifier really clean and is it worth it, right? So when I'm thinking
about this here, I'm now kind of, I'm looking at the different products, right? I'm aware of the air
purifier, but I'm trying to figure out if it's the right product for me, right? So how much space can
an air purifier really clean, right? Very, very logistical question. And is it actually worth it? So if I
saw an ad as a product aware person looking for an air purifier, and I was, I had, you know, an ad
that basically handled some of these questions, that would definitely help convert, right? Now we
climb the ladder again to solution aware, okay? Is ultraviolet cleaning a better long-term way to
keep the air in my home clean, right? So this is somebody who is now aware of different solutions
to the air purifier.
And again, if you remember that ad that I showcased in the market awareness level or in that, in
that presentation, it was actually comparing that slide board to the Peloton, right? So this could
be a solution aware of somebody, again, looking for a better way to, to learn weight or burn or
lose weight or burn calories, calories is, you know, basically Peloton, you know, the right, right
way to lose or the best way to lose weight. Actually, no. Okay.
It's, it's going to be the slide board instead. Right? Same thing with the air purifier here is
ultraviolet cleaning a better long-term way to keep the air in my home clean. Then we climb the
ladder once more, right? I feel congested at home, but not at work.
What in the world is going on? Okay. Again, this, as you can imagine, is going to start to give you
ad ideas for your product. And how do we, you know, basically attack this? It's like feeling
congested at home and don't know why, or curious of why, you know, you like your stroke, like,
you know, breathing seems harder at night or whatever.
Like can't struggling to breathe that night, whatever it is, right? There we go. We can start to
identify that problem. And again, remember, work them down into the solution, right? All the way

to the actual product.
And then finally, unaware, right? Exercise and health are important to me. Besides doing cardio,
how else can I improve my cardiovascular health, right? People aren't even aware that an air
purifier can actually help with cardiovascular health. But in this instance, again, as you can see,
we're able to kind of create ads that resonate with these people.
Okay. Work all the way down the list as well. If you obviously are not taking care of your
cardiovascular health, it can lead to congestion at home, which is why you need to be aware of
the solution.
The air purifier work all the way down. And here's a great offer. Okay.
So that is how you can leverage market awareness. This is not easy. Okay.
So it's not something that you can expect to just come in and know the exact questions that your
customers are asking at each stage and have answers for every single one of them. But I would
definitely just encourage you to try to ask a few different questions here. Okay.

know, unaware might be, you know, I really, you know, I see these girls with beautiful, shiny hair.
Wow.

FILE: 19_ Shaun Eng on X Tons of Ecom brands will tell you they have a 20_ winning ad hit r.txt

---
title: "(19) Shaun Eng on X: \"Tons of Ecom brands will tell you they have a \"20% winning ad hit rate\".Yet their revenue stays flat.⠀Everyone in Ecom is obsessed with winning ads. You tell your team you need more winners. They make more ads. Some hit KPI. Some get spend. You think you're doing well, https://t.co/yZ096DVHoJ\""
source: "https://x.com/shauneng/status/2064814867530617332"
author:
published: 2025-09-22
created: 2026-06-28
description:
tags:
  - "clippings"
---
## Post

## Conversation[Shaun Eng](https://x.com/shauneng)[@shauneng](https://x.com/shauneng)

Tons of Ecom brands will tell you they have a "20% winning ad hit rate". Yet their revenue stays flat. ⠀ Everyone in Ecom is obsessed with winning ads. You tell your team you need more winners. They make more ads. Some hit KPI. Some get spend. You think you're doing well, but revenue stays the same. ⠀ Because your definition of "winning ad" isn't moving the needle. ⠀ Winning ads exist on a spectrum. These 4 variables decide how winning an ad actually is: ⠀ 1) KPI ⠀ Target CAC/ROAS. The better the ad performs against target, the more winning it is. This is where most of you stop reading your data. It's 1 of 4. ⠀ 2) Spend % ⠀ What share of your budget is this ad eating? Meta votes with spend. When it likes an ad, it feeds it. When it doesn't, it starves it. The more spend the ad captures, the more winning it is. ⠀ (CBO only. In ABO, judge the ad's share of total account spend while it's still at KPI.) ⠀ 3) Time ⠀ If your ad has only run for 2 days, it tells you almost nothing. 14+ days tells you it's actually working. The longer the ad performs, the more winning it is. ⠀ 4) Scale ⠀ Did the ad grow your total daily spend? Not just take budget from other ads. Not just "hit $2k spend at KPI." Actually push the whole account higher. ⠀ Most of your ads won't peak on all four. That doesn't make them losers. But the label you put on an ad determines what you do next. Wrong label, wrong decision. Every time. ⠀ The 4 labels: ⠀ Breakthrough ads: High spend % AND scaling your total daily spend at KPI. Not just taking budget from other creatives. Actually grows the whole account. Rare. When you find one, you'll know. ⠀ That's why guys like Gulfam and Spencer can make 1 dialled-in ad concept a day and still scale to $100k days. Their Breakthrough rate is high. They don't need volume. ⠀ Spend Winner: High spend %, but the account isn't growing. Meta backs it, people aren't buying. Usually a misleading hook, misaligned audience targeting in creative, or not enough belief built to convert. ⠀ KPI Winner: Converts, but Meta won't spend more on it. Hook's too narrow. Force more spend and KPIs collapse, because Meta doesn't know who to go after. ⠀ Loser: Not hitting KPI. Not getting spend. ⠀ This is why your "20-30% hit rate" means nothing. Most of you are treating Spend Winners like Breakthroughs and calling KPI Winners "wins" because they hit your targets. ⠀ Meanwhile your Breakthrough rate is near zero. No wonder the brand isn't scaling. ⠀ Your Breakthrough hit rate is the number you should be optimizing for. Be real with yourself, because it's not as high as you think - else you'd be mega scaling MoM. ⠀ Few.

[![Image](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HKemB4DaoAAMOSR?format=jpg&name=small)](https://x.com/shauneng/status/2064814867530617332/photo/1)

Quote

Shaun Eng

@shauneng

Sep 22, 2025

"Test More Creatives" is bad advice for someone trying to scale their Ecom brand Student A: Testing 50-70 creatives/week, stuck at $300k/month, no winning ad in months Student B: Testing 5-10 concepts/week, doing $100k days The difference? Hit rate. 100 creatives at 1% hit

[![Image](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/G1b_cUTXgAAMUu1?format=jpg&name=small)](https://x.com/shauneng/status/1970157574550167947/photo/1)

2. IMPORTANT - How To See If You Qualify For This
Alright, so welcome to the overview of the customer feedback survey. Now, for those of you that
have not heard of this before, this is a pretty cool system that we actually created on our own. It's
kind of like our, I don't even know how we did it, but myself and Ash, who used to work at the
agency as well, has now gone on to do awesome things.
Basically, we kind of created this and it was a great way for us to collect a bunch of feedback
from our customers while actually kind of making money in the process as well, because people
typically buy from emails. And we've turned this into now a system that really helps us just
ultimately better understand the customer, which again, when it comes to customer feedback
survey, what makes it so valuable is you're able to understand the type of people that you attract
specifically with your marketing and everything else that you're doing. But more importantly, you
understand why people bought from you specifically.
And that right there can be the little piece that you need to really break through and start creating
more winning ads. And so that's why customer feedback survey data is so valuable. Now, with
that said, this is not going to be for every single one of you, okay? And obviously, if you're a
newer brand or you don't have as many customers to send to, that's why I have all of these
spreadsheets pulled up.
I'm going to go ahead and show you the different size of list and the results that we got. And then
from there, you can make the decision on whether or not this is something that you want to take
the time to go through and set up. And I will start this off by saying as well, is that if you have a
crappy product, or you have been drop shipping something that people are not happy about, and
you're having to constantly hide your reviews, you should not do this.
What you should do is you should go and fix your product. And then you will likely start to actually
scale further as well, rather than just trying to sell hunks of crap, okay? I understand people want
to make money, I totally get it. But at the end of the day, if you're just selling crap, then this is just
going to be a waste of time for you.
And that should be a sign that you probably need to fix your product, okay? If you have a great
product, it makes everything else in life easy. So that's what I would recommend that you do.
Now, with that said, to give you an example of how many customers you might need in order to
actually have this entire process that I'm about to show you be worth it, I have data pulled from
over 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 different surveys that we've done.
We've done over 30 of these surveys. But just to give you an idea of the kind of entries that we
get for the list size, I have this pulled up here as well, okay? Now, for example, here is a client
that sells a product that is kind of like a one and done product, okay? Once you buy it, you don't
really need to buy it again. And they ran a survey and their offer was to give you another one of

those products, okay? The winner could win another one of those products.
Now, with that said, keep in mind their product is worth over $350. And so for some people, that's
kind of valuable. But to show you the data, we had 286 or 2,861 people receive the email, okay?
So these are people in Klaviyo, not just total customers, but like validated emails in Klaviyo,

language for the first half and then got into sourcing.

working with daily. I worked with about 20 factories because it was more of a store brand and
was getting to the point by 2018 that I decided I wanted to do sourcing for e-commerce
businesses.

know necessarily the difference of what they're working with. So I think in this module, you know,
I think we'll do a good job just like breaking it down for you all so that you kind of know what
bucket you're in or maybe you might want to reconsider the bucket you're currently in.
So we're going to get the party started. Yeah. So for factories, we've bucketed into two main
factories that we're going to go over today.
One of them is going to be raw material factories. So raw material factories are going to be the
factories that are responsible for producing all of the individual components that go into actually
making a product, right? If you're going to be injecting plastic or making bottles, if you're going to
be using metal components or batteries or PCBs, things like that, the electronics, the actual
individual components that need to get procured at the assembly factory, we're just going to
classify those as raw material factories for this presentation. Moving on to the other category of
factories we're going to talk about is going to be assembly factories.
And so what assembly factories do is they assemble your product. They're going to get all of the
raw materials, individual components from sub vendors, and they're going to perform the QC.
They're going to do the final assembly.
They're going to do the final packaging, and they're going to be the ones that you would work
directly with most of the time to get a product assembled. Starting with the advantages, when
you're working with a factory, you're going to be working with the direct source. And what that
means is they're going to be the ones ultimately assembling the product.
They're usually the ones with the engineers or designers and people that are going to be
designing the product. They're very specialized in that niche or category of products that they
manufacture. So you're going direct.
You're going to get access to a lot of really valuable resources with going direct to the factory.
They're going to usually have lower pricing than working with a trade company or a drop shipping
agent, and they will probably have higher MOQs. Their business is running a factory, so they
need to make sure that the assembly line workers have stuff to do, the purchasing team, the QC
team.
They need to make sure that the lines are filled all the time, so they're going to be looking for the
higher volume orders. You have a chance to collaborate with them on product creation and
product customization. So as I said earlier, they have the engineering team.
They have the design team. They have all the resources you would need to go and customize a
product with them or start doing a product creation with them. There's a few different size
factories to keep in mind.

We've categorized them into small, medium, and large. So there are some advantages to
different size factories that we're going to talk about. So small factories are going to be a little bit
more eager for the business.
Usually they're kind of in startup mode. They're just going to be getting started, and they're going
to be looking for any type of business that they can get that's in their specialty, right? So small
factories are great if you're just getting started, if you want someone to give you extra special
attention, but their resources might be a little bit limited, right? The engineers might be a little bit
busy. You might have to wait a little bit longer for production times and things.
So there's a bit of a give and take, and we'll go into that a little bit more in the disadvantages.
Medium size factories, you're just going to get a little bit more resources, and then you're going to
get a little bit better pricing, but still just a factory. And then for larger factories, you're going to get
very extensive resources in a much higher capacity.
If you need to develop a lot of products, if you need a lot of attention to what you've got going on,
and you need a really high capacity because you're scaling and your volume is going crazy,
you're going to maybe want to look for a larger factory. So let's get into the disadvantages of
working direct with a factory. Firstly, quality control can be a little bit difficult to manage on your
own.
Working with a factory, they're going to be set in their ways a little bit. They're going to have their
own standard operating procedures for how they do production, how they do quality control. And
if you have some special requirements for the quality or production of your product, then it might
be a little bit difficult to communicate that with them and then to go in and kind of change their
ways and make sure that they're doing things the way you want them to be done.
So it just takes a lot of time. It takes a lot of time and a lot of setup on your own end to be able to
get quality control going at a factory. Factories also only specialize in products that are in one
vertical usually or one category.
So that means if a factory makes cell phones, then they only really make cell phones. You're not
going to find an electronics factory that's making Bluetooth headsets and cell phones and
cameras and all of these things. They're just assembly shops for the most part.
And so if you want to do a range of products, then you're going to need to work with a range of
factories. You can't really just find like a one-stop shop factory that can make everything that
you're trying to do. Low-volume ordering with the factory is also going to make you not as much
of a priority.
If your volume isn't very high, you're not driving a lot of business to the factory, then they might
not really care so much about you as a customer or your orders. And so it can kind of get a little
weird trying to grow with a factory if you're not bringing a lot of value to the table depending on

challenges.
A lot of factories aren't going to have robust English speakers. They might not have their sales

So show them how it works. All right. So if you're going to create a custom consumer electronics
product, the first thing you're going to want to do is identify your product concept, right? That
means, you know, what, what kind of product are you going to do? You're gonna want to make an
outline about the product, right? What, what product is it? What is it going to do? What are some
of the features? How is it going to be designed? And then you're going to want to sketch it out.
The, the, the sketch is going to be one of the most important parts of this, because as you're
sketching it out, you're going to kind of be able to solve some of the problems that you might not
have thought about until you, you know, put the pen to the paper and really start to figure out
some of the details and things you're going to run into certain obstacles and things. And so you're
going to really have to think through it in terms of features and designs and like how you're going
to achieve what you're trying to achieve. After you get the product concept and outline together
the next step there's actually two steps you're going to want to do simultaneously here and it's
going to start with an industrial design, right? So you've got your, your sketch and your outline
and your features and designs all pinned down.
So what the industrial design is going to be is the actual shape and form factor of the, of the
device, right? How's it going to look? How, how's it going to be shaped? How's it going to, you

know, fit in the hand, those types of things. And so to get started on the industrial design, you're
going to want to find a designer, if you're not capable of doing the design yourself which can be a
little bit expensive you might be able to use a factory to do it which is kind of why you want to do
the due diligence part, finding the factory, seeing what's out there in tandem with this step and
then you can kind of decide like, Hey, do I need to find someone, you know, third party to
outsource this type of thing or do I want to just use a factory? Maybe you find a factory in your
due diligence phase that like has a bunch of products in that range that you think are great. And
so you say, Hey, like maybe we just work on this and we changed it a little bit and you can work
on, work on it with their team of designers and engineers.
I think also too, if you go the factory route for them to be doing the industrial design for you, like
this is where you do need to be a little cautious because you need to be probably working with
like a patent attorney or to speak to the factory upfront about it because like you do want to
protect your IP. If that like kind of goes unspoken and you're not talking about doing a design
patent or figuring that out, you know, plan of attack with a lawyer, you know, you could end up
having a factory do all the ID, create everything for you. And now, you know, they own your
patent in China.
So you can't switch factories if, you know, the relationship gets sour or something like that. So I
just want to throw that in there. You know, if you are creating custom product, like, you know, just
be aware that you probably should be consulting with an attorney when you're going through this.
But yeah, that's just a side note. I'll let you go. Yeah.
And so, I mean, that's, that's going to be part of the due diligence assessment phase here where
you're just going to want to find a factory that's a good fit for you. You're going to want to find a
factory that you can trust and work with. This is regardless if you work with them on the design or
not, you know, you just want to make sure that it's a good fit.
And, you know, we'll go over in a different module about how to specifically do due diligence on a
factory and figure out if they're a good fit or not. But that's one of the things to consider when,
when you're doing the due diligence phase. So after you've kind of pinned down a factory and
you've, you've got your design kind of figured out, you know, you're going to start to be able to get
estimated project costs, right? That's going to be, you know, how long the development phase is
going to take and like how much that's going to cost you.
And then also like a solid estimate on what the actual price per unit is going to be in the end. So
after you've identified all of these pieces, you're going to want to go into more of the structural
design aspect of the product. This is going to be getting your 3D files ready, you know, in the
form of a CAD file, STP files, stuff like that.
And what that means is, you know, the structural design differs in the industrial design where

you're going to actually figure out where like all the nuts and bolts are going to go, what pieces
are going to need to be glued on. You're going to figure out what type of finishes and materials
you're going to do. You're going to also start to figure out like what your logo placement is going
to be like, and then the branding on your product.
How are you going to make it unique? How are you going to make it stylized to your brand and
what you're trying to do? Once you get your files set, the next part is going to be prototyping,
right? Which is making your samples. So there's a few different ways to go about prototyping.
One of the more simple ways is going to be a 3D print.
So a 3D print is going to be a lot faster and a lot cheaper than making other different types of
prototypes. And that's because, you know, you can just use a 3D printer to kind of print out the
shape and the form factor. And then that's going to allow you to kind of feel and hold the product
and see if it's the right size, the right weight, if it feels right in the hand, like those types of things.
Then once you kind of get that figured out, you might want to go into an actual CNC prototype. So
a CNC prototype is going to actually be, you know, cutting plastics, cutting metals. Maybe there's
like some textile or some silicone componentry to it as well.
But that's going to be more closely representing the final product that you're trying to make, like
what a mass production unit is going to look like. And you're actually going to be able to start
doing some testing and stuff with that to make sure that you're actually able to achieve the
function that you're trying to roll out with this product. So the sampling phase can take a few
cycles to get right.
Once you create the sample, you're going to want to do some testing. You're going to want to
create your checklist to make sure that it hits all the boxes for everything that you're trying to do
with this product concept. So if you don't get it right the first time, don't get discouraged.
A lot of times you're going to have to do some refinements and then, you know, go into second
and third rounds of sampling. It's totally common. So once you finally have a sample that you're
happy with, you've evaluated it for all of the different things that you're trying to consider and you
know that like, Hey, okay, so this is going to check all my boxes.
This is going to do everything that I want it to do. And the cost estimates are, you know, in line
with what you're trying to do. Then you're going to start getting into the tooling aspect, which is
one of the more complex areas of making a custom product.
So what tooling is, is making the molds for the product, right? And so most of the componentry
that you're going to be using is going to require a mold. What a mold is, is something that allows
you to, you know, mass produce the components, right? Maybe the mold is like one big steel
casing that produces like eight plastic parts that are identical at a time. Oftentimes you can
actually get some like ancillary products.

If you're doing like a plastic mold, like you could do like the casing and the internal frame of the
product, maybe in one mold or something like that. So there's these huge steel like things that are
just molds, right? They're like, like big cookie cutters, if you will, right. To just kind of mass
produce a product, you're going to want to evaluate the tooling costs, right? You're going to, at
this point, you're going to start to understand how many components are in the product, how
many pieces you're going to need to get the job done.
And you're going to start to figure out what the tooling costs are. And you're going to want to
evaluate like, Hey, is this investment really something that's right for you? Because of the tooling,
it takes a long time. It's quite a large investment.
We're going to get into the cost of that here in the next slide or the next few slides. But at this
point is like really your final, like go, no go stage for if you're going to do this product or not. You
know, once you get the sample, you're going to see the tooling costs.
You're going to understand the price per unit costs. And this is like your final, are you, are you
going to jump in this pool or not? Right. Um, once, once you've gotten tooling together, um, you
start opening the molds, like your product is locked at that point.
There's not going to be any changes and that's really what you're going to go to market with. So
once the product is locked, then you can go and start figuring out what the packaging is going to
look like. You're going to have the, uh, overall product design.
You're going to know what size it is. And then you're going to be able to get dye lines for the
product. So once you have the dye lines for the product, you're actually going to be able to start
working on the packaging.
So when you're doing the packaging, you're going to want to start figuring out like, okay, how am I
going to brand this product? How, how am I going to package it? What kind of packaging offering
is that going to look like? How am I going to ship it? How am I going to brand it? You know, all, all
of those different things. And since tooling is going to take you, you know, six weeks to eight
weeks, uh, roughly, you're going to have plenty of time to go ahead and get your packaging
designed, you're going to be able to run packaging samples. So that way, by the time the tooling
is finished and you have your finished product sampling, you're going to have packaging to go
along with it.
So that takes us right into the next step of, uh, your finished product sampling. So these come in
the form of trial production runs. So what a trial production run is, is once the tooling is done, you
have the mass production quality units coming out, um, from tooling.
And at that point is when you're going to be able to evaluate it to make sure that everything is
perfect and you're going to start to be able to get your, uh, quality control SOPs in place. So how
to get your quality control SOPs in place is you're going to want to figure out all of the different

aspects that you want the factory to look at when they're performing QC on your product, um,
from the incoming QC for what the raw materials are coming in and what they look like and how
they're evaluating it to how it's being assembled to, uh, you know, the final checks before it goes
into packaging as well as like the QC for the packaging, right? Um, is the material thick enough?
Is the printing good? Like, are the colors proper? All of these different things are going to be
identified here in the finished product sampling phase. Um, you can also take these finished
product samples, uh, to go and do any certifications you might need.
Like if you're going to do FDA certificates, um, CE rose certificates, any, any of those, you know,
certificates that you need, depending on what market you're selling into, you're going to want to
use the finished product sampling to go and do that because if you're not using a finished product
and like there are some small changes after you go into mass production, then the certifications
are going to become a null and void. So you want to make sure that you're using the finished
product samples to go and do that. And then this also gives you an opportunity to get your
marketing samples done.
So if you want to do some influencer seeding, you want to start getting some UGC up, uh, this is
an opportunity before you go into mass production to go ahead and get a set of units that you can
go and start doing the marketing with. Um, so after you've checked all the boxes off here, you've
got your QC SOP in place. You've got all the certificates you need.
You've got your marketing samples. You've got your packaging figured out. Your tooling is done.
Um, now you're ready to go into mass production. So what that looks like is you're going to want
to understand what the raw material procurement process is like. Um, there's going to be your,
your lead times for that.
Each different material is going to have a different lead time. There's a lot of different stuff to
coordinate at this point. Um, so you're going to want to start to forecast like, okay, what is your
first order actually going to look like? How fast are you going to sell through it? When are you
going to put your next order in? Because once you have the lead times for the product and you
understand what the mass production phase is going to look like, you can start to really figure out
what your order planning is going to look like based on your sales forecasts.
Um, so then the final step is going to be placing the order for mass production. Once you place
the order, all the raw materials are going to start to get produced. Um, and then they're going to
start coming into the factory for final assembly.
So as the factory is receiving the parts for final assembly, they're going to be performing QC on
the parts before they come in and make sure everything's coming in the right size, the right
shape. It's going to be a good fitment and that's before they go into assembly. Then once they
start assembling things, there's more QC going on.

Um, and then finally, you're going to just get into the, the packing and shipping aspect of it. After
mass production, um, there is a quality control aspect of it where you're going to want to make
sure that your quality standards are in place. You're going to want to develop an SOP with the
factory.
If you're able to go and like see the assembly line and see the actual production, that's going to
help a lot because you're going to be able to identify some things that maybe you didn't think
about. Maybe the factory isn't thinking about. Um, so it's really important to be able to go and do
spot checks, whether you're on the ground yourself, or if you have someone to go and do those
types of spot checks for you, uh, that happens, you know, towards the end of the mass
production phase.
So these two steps kind of happen in tandem as well. Now, after you're done with mass
production, after you've got your quality control in place, now you want to start trying to look at
some cost reduction solutions so that you can go ahead and scale with this product that you just
created. Right.
So what that looks like is a lot of optimizing. You're going to want to go and look at these lead
times you got for your raw materials again and understand like, okay, um, how many more do I
need to buy to be able to get my price down? Uh, maybe you need to buy certain things ahead of
time to be able to get your lead time shorter. Right.
For example, with, with consumer electronics, oftentimes the longest lead time part is going to be
the batteries, right? So, you know, maybe the batteries 45 days, but all the other plastic parts and
electronic components take only 20 days. So it's over twice as long just to get your batteries in.
And so you're going to want to figure out like, okay, maybe I should be placing, you know, battery
orders a little bit sooner than I'm placing orders for other components.
And then you're going to want to go ahead and talk with the factory about it. Like, okay, at what
point am I going to start to see discounts if I go in place or, you know, larger volume orders for
some of these components. Right.
And then line that up with your forecasting too. And then go ahead and see if like, you can maybe
put a deposit down for a little bit more of the materials. And then you can set up like some batch
delivery type of scenarios.
Um, and that really kind of optimizes the lead times with your forecasting that just helps you
scale. Right. So as you're scaling, you just want to make sure that you are communicative with
the factory about what you're doing to scale or not necessarily what you're doing to scale, but like
how you're scaling.
Um, and then what your plans are to scale over the, you know, the next months and year so that
they can work with you on optimizing these, uh, you know, cost reduction, opportune, and then,

Now I didn't get into the factory part of it too much, like with the formula suppliers, but when

you want each of those factories to be giving you like anywhere from two to five variations,
because that's how you're going to be finding a winning formula. Okay. And you're also going to
figure out who the best factory partner is, right? Because when you send, when you get these
samples, like what you are measuring and testing is the scent, the texture and effectiveness,
right? Like that's how you rank the variations.
That's how you're going to rank the, you know, as far as you just get yourself a spreadsheet, do

go from like five factories to two factories. And like this gives you the best chance to get started
the fastest.
If you work with one factory, think about it. What if you don't like any of the samples? What if,
what if they're not very good with scent, right? They're good with texture, but they're not good with
scent. Now it's going to smell chemically and you're gonna be like, Oh my God, this factory sucks.
Well, that's not how this all works, right? So it's more just about like work with more of them. And

could be additional sampling, right? You might have your winning formula from maybe two

factories and you want to improve the texture or you want to improve the scent.

We're all like, just like trying to make money, right? We're trying to like, go, go, go scale to the

So, um, then the transparency, like the more transparent a, um, formula factory is like the better,

If they're not up, there's a very good chance you ended up working with a trade company. Or it's
just a factory. That's like, like not willing to be as collaborative as, as you'd like.
You know, like when, when we get pricing on skincare, like we don't just send them like one price.
I could like, because then you're not going to know how to lower it over time. You have various
costs.
You have your box packaging costs, which would go from a box packaging factory. You have
your bottle costs, which goes from a bottle factory. You got your formula costs, which would be
broken into two, your, your processing costs and your material costs.
Processing is like the assembly of everything and getting it together. Right. So you see like all

those costs, you know, when you're, when you're getting it from a formula factory, if the formula
factory is doing your bottle sourcing, then they'll give you your bottle price, but they're putting a
margin on that, right.
Cause they're not producing the bottle themselves. The same thing with a box. If you have, if you
have the formula factory getting the box for you, they're adding a margin on the box.
You know, that's why for a company like us, we're working directly with the factories and that's
how we're able to stay competitive with formula factories. Actually. Like we actually compete on
price with the formula factories because.
Like on bottles, they will charge a margin because like they, you're giving them more of the
responsibility. There's an operation expense in that for them. Right? Yeah.
Exactly. Exactly. So, um, you know, that's just like the stuff to be thinking about.
Then lastly, you got your certificates. You, I mean, you should be doing this from the very
beginning, making sure, like if you're in the U S market, you need FDA certificates. If you're in
Europe, you need GMPC.
But then if you want to go a step further, especially for Europe, because we do work with like
brands that only target Europe, you need a microbiology test, a challenge test, a stability test, and
a compatibility test. Compatibility meaning with the bottle and the formula, because you don't
want a chemical reaction of the formula going into the bottle. Right? So now those tests you don't
get right away.
You, you know, it takes time even after you've produced your order, but you just need to know
from the factory that that's something that they do. If they don't provide those tests, I don't think
they're really as legitimate, even if they have an FDA certificate. So you do want to make sure
they're buttoned up because when it's time for you to bring your brand to something like Amazon,
or if you've scaled and you're doing like eight figures, someone's asking for that stuff, you know,
and you just want to be able to hand it to them, be like, cool, done.
I'm doing, doing my thing. Let me, let me mark it and scale. Right.

4.2 The Desire Calendar - Part 2 - Evolve Evolve
All right, we are going to be talking about the desire calendar, and this is something that I don't
think a lot of people have ever seen. I don't even know if this actually exists, but this is pretty cool
because what this actually does is it tells you essentially what desires are peaking and what
people are feeling during the year. And this is why so many brands struggle and, you know, for
example, do really, really, really well in December and can absolutely crush during December.
And then January comes and it absolutely sucks. And then, you know, February, they're getting
wrecked. Or for example, in months like July and August, they're just getting totally destroyed
because they don't really know what to do.
And so that's why we created this calendar. And this is something that we have started using to
be able to just know, hey, this is off of literal biology of like what, for example, even the sun does
to people, right, like during the year. And this is a study that actually a big credit to Alan Soltanik, I
think his name is.
He did a study on this to see like, OK, people shop and behave way differently during the
summer because they get essentially more dopamine. And compared to the winter when there's
less dopamine because we're not in the sun all day and we're not happy, we crave dopamine.
And so we make a lot of smaller purchases during the winter months.
And obviously, you have to buy gifts and stuff like that. But during the summer months, we tend
to make bigger purchases like going on vacation or buying new cars. And so it's very interesting,
at least for you to be aware of this.
If you're kind of just starting out, don't let this overcomplicate you. This is obviously more for those
of you that are trying to understand, hey, maybe you have bad months. How can you break
through that? So I'm not going to really go through each and every single one, but I do just want
to call across a few and also start with, you know, where we're at right now and where we're
about to go into, I would say, because this is really, really important to understand for Q4.
And then also kind of take a look at, you know, some other months as well from you guys who
maybe have been struggling in July and August and maybe help you figure out why. So just know
that right now, I mean, it's September. So what does September look like? Well, September from
a desire standpoint is really about achievement and structure desires.
Okay. And so I want you guys to understand, like, this is a deep research that we did through AI. I

process of how to reduce your defect rates and improve your quality, et cetera. So the first thing
you're gonna wanna do is make sure that your customer service team is properly trained, right?
So that means that when your customer service team is talking to your customers and they're
getting the feedback that they are identifying and tracking your defect rates, okay? Secondly,
we're gonna talk about suppliers with defective products and how you can recover some of the
lost revenue.
Then we're gonna go over some examples that you can use now to improve your product quality.
Then we're gonna go over your biggest supply chain issues from stockouts to defect rates and
just maintaining inventory flow. Your customer service team and how they can help you identify
and track these defect rates.
So what you're gonna wanna do is ensure that your team can troubleshoot properly. This is a
huge first step because a lot of issues can be just user error, right? Maybe it's not a defect with
the product. Maybe there's a missing piece of education to how to use it.
Maybe the expectations aren't clear. So a lot of this can be dealt with just with proper
troubleshooting and a good customer service process, right? So you wanna make sure that your
team is ready and patient to educate the end user. That means that the customer service team
understands the product fully.
They know exactly how to troubleshoot it and then they understand what the actual defects are
so that if a customer comes in with something that's maybe a user error, then they're not
immediately going to refund the product or accept a return, but that they're actually willing to go
and educate and make sure that the end user knows how to use the product. Then lastly, with the
customer service teams, you wanna just make sure that they're only refunding and replacing
products when it's necessary after they've already deemed that it's a real defect. So what you're
gonna wanna do with the customer service team is narrow down the issues with your product into
specific buckets, right? So some examples of that could be dead on arrival for like an electronics

product, or if it's not charging, maybe it's showing up with broken components or maybe it's just
not functioning as expected, right? So these are all just like some broad buckets that you can go
ahead and put these defect rates into after you've determined that it's not a user error, right? So
you're gonna wanna focus on these real defects and not the user issues because the factories
are not gonna recognize user errors.
They're gonna wanna educate you on how to properly educate the customer and then they're not
gonna be able to compensate you for anything that isn't actually deemed a manufacturer's defect.
So you're gonna wanna learn how to educate the customer to give them the best experience with
your product. And then you're gonna wanna keep the reporting clean that you're sharing with the
supplier so that you're not taking things up the chain that are actually user errors, right? You're
gonna wanna make it nice and clean and specific to things that are actually deemed as
manufacturer's defect and you can work with your supplier on getting that figured out.
The best way to do these reports is a weekly, monthly and quarterly report. This will help you
track defect rates as they're coming in. This will help you track improvements and then it'll also
help you track new defects, right? So the report can be shared with the factory for insights and
improvements.
They're gonna be able to take a look at it and let you know if you're troubleshooting properly or
not. They're gonna be able to help you identify new issues and new solutions. You're gonna
wanna make sure that the data is specific and easy to read.
You don't wanna give them some huge convoluted Excel sheet with all this crazy information on it
because that's not helpful for anyone. So you wanna make sure that it's easy to read and it's
easily digestible for the supplier when you're sharing it with them. And then you're gonna wanna
use this report to go ahead and track the improvement progress.
Again, on a weekly basis, monthly basis and a quarterly basis, you're gonna wanna see those
numbers going down over time as you're working with the supplier on these. So next we're gonna
go into how you can recover some lost revenue due to defective products with your supplier. So
what you're gonna wanna do is from the very beginning, you're gonna wanna negotiate a
warranty policy with the manufacturer.
And you're gonna wanna understand exactly what they do and do not cover as far as a warranty
and what they do and do not consider a manufacturer defect. So once you get those kind of
buckets identified with the supplier, you're gonna wanna work with them on making a report that
they are gonna be able to accept for warranty purposes. The last thing you want is like, you end
up deeming something a defect on your own, you haven't gone through the due diligence with the
factory to get their agreement on it.
And then now you're sending over this report and then they're saying like, oh, well, you need to
send samples over for assessment or whatever, because now you're just wasting a lot of time.

You don't wanna have to deal with sending samples back and forth to try to prove your point to
them. You just wanna let them know like, hey, this is what's happening.
How do you want me to record this information so that you believe that this issue is actually
occurring so that you can go ahead and compensate me for it. Then you're gonna wanna agree
on fair terms for what manufacturers are gonna cover monetarily for you, right? For example, are
they gonna give you the full price of your sale, including your shipping and including your cost of
acquisition and whatever your marketing cost, operation cost is, or are they just gonna give you a
product replacement or something in between? So this is something you're gonna wanna
negotiate upfront so that you're not just coming to them at the end of a quarter with like a huge list
of stuff that's saying like, now you want all this money from them and like you're just kind of
coming out of left field. It gives them a lot to think about and it can be a little bit overwhelming and
it's gonna not be good for your success rate of trying to get some lost revenue recovered here.
Another thing you can do with your factory is try to see if they will be willing to overship quantities
based on your QC issues. So what that means is, for example, if you are seeing a 5% defect rate,
maybe on your next order, they're gonna be able to overship by 5% on what your actual order
was so that you can be ready to deal with that expected defect rate going on while they're trying
to figure out a solution for you. Another thing you could maybe do is double dip a little bit, where if
you have that 5% defect rate, you get a 5% credit on your next order and they're gonna overship
by 5%.
So that's like a best case scenario. Obviously, the best case scenario actually would be like no
defects, but if you are getting defects, like maybe that's something you wanna talk about with
your supplier to try to get compensated for it. So it's important to keep an open mind for different
warranty terms, different products, different suppliers.
They're all gonna have a different philosophy on how to deal with it. Some factories are only
gonna offer you credit on the next order for the product. They're gonna try to keep you in their
ecosystem.
And then they might be really great to work with on trying to figure out these defects. So that
could be a good option for you. You also wanna be willing to send any defective products back to
the manufacturer, because a lot of times it is going to need to get sent back for analysis,
especially if it's like a new issue or a new product or something that they haven't seen before.
And then you always wanna remain solution oriented when you're talking with them about it. Don't
just rely on them for solutions because that's just gonna be slow. They also don't understand your
market.
They don't understand your customer. And so it's always best to go to them with some sort of

5 Understanding Market Sophistication - Part 1 - Evolve Evolve
Alright, so in this video, we're going to be talking about market sophistication. Now, I want to be
100% clear that I'm going to try to make this as simple and as easy as possible to understand for
you, because I know that a lot of people, when they first hear market sophistication, they're like,
what is this? But this is essentially going to help you understand why e-commerce is so hard,
okay, or why creating winning ads is really hard. Like, if you don't understand this, it is extremely
hard to compete in sophisticated markets, okay? And if you've ever heard the word saturated,
that's essentially what market sophistication is.
But we're going to break it down today. I'm going to make it very easy and very simple for you to
understand. And ultimately, again, remember that we're understanding these things piece by
piece, product, desire, market sophistication, we're going to understand avatars, we're going to
understand angles.
And then again, I'm going to put all of this together. But let's go ahead and jump to the screen to
get started with helping you clearly understand. And even if you've seen the other training on
market sophistication, you should 100% watch this because it is going to make it so much easier
for you to process and know how to actually apply this to your ads, or better yet, how to actually
just be aware of it when writing your ads.
All right, so as you can see here, why you need to understand market sophistication before
creating an avatar. Well, again, if you don't know, this is Eugene Schwartz, you know, shocker.
It's his theory.
And he actually created this theory, as you can see here, in 1966. And it is actually still relevant
today. That's what I love so much about marketing is if you actually get to the fundamentals and
to the core of marketing, a lot of this stuff still applies, right? You have the product, you have the
desires of what people want, you have the sophistication levels, you have the specific people that
you're going after, you have the angle, essentially how you're going after them, right? So all of
this stuff still applies.
It's just the vehicle in which we apply it. Maybe back 50 years ago, it was newspapers, or it was,
you know, magazines, or it was direct mail, where now it's on social media, okay? But it still
applies, okay? And so the one thing that's important for everybody to understand is that the rate
at which these markets are sophisticating is now faster than ever. And the reason why is
because, you know, 50 years ago, it would take word of mouth to essentially reach others, you
know, and tell others that the product just wasn't that great or that, you know, you had a bad
experience with it.
And even then, that would just require you actually telling someone about it, right? So for
example, you know, if I buy these, you know, this gum, I don't know, I can't remember where I got

this, but it's plant-based gum, okay? And if I had a bad experience with this gum, and I just didn't
like it, I'm probably not going to go like tell people like, oh, make sure you don't get, you know,
refresh gum. It was actually good gum. So I don't know whose brand this is, but at the end of the
day, I'm not going to go out really and tell people like, oh yeah, I had this gum the other day and it
was terrible.
It's not really something that I'm going to like bring up and tell a lot of people, especially go to a
stranger on the street and be like, yeah, don't buy this gum, right? You're just not going to do that.
And 50 years ago, it's kind of what it was like, right? So if people had a bad experience with the
product, they'd just be like, nah, I mean, obviously if someone asked me like, hey, have you tried
this gum? And I might be like, yeah, it's not that good. Then obviously word of mouth gets around
that way.
But nowadays you have to understand that as global commerce, e-commerce has essentially,
you know, grown and blown up the, you know, the opportunity to scale what you can sell and sell
globally has been, you know, it's, it's the biggest ever been, it's the easiest it's ever been. But you
have to understand. So, and thankfully, so has customer feedback and word of mouth, right?
Nowadays, people can make a TikTok and make an Instagram real saying like, you know, this is
literally the worst gum I've ever had in my life.
Right. And as a result, now everybody is just posting and like millions of people can see it, right?
Like millions. And so you markets get sophisticated extremely, extremely fast.
And there's also platforms like Reddit where people can Google, hey, what is this product like?
And everybody's saying, oh, don't buy it. It's terrible. It's horrible.
You know, don't get it. Right. So you have to understand that markets are sophisticating at a rate
that they never have before, which is why a lot of people are very secretive with what they're
doing in e-commerce.
And on top of that, it's the reason why we see people, you know, start an e-commerce brand and
they scale to, you know, a couple of hundred thousand dollars a month, right. During, you know,
maybe November, December, or maybe they get a product early. And this is also just as a quick
side tangent, why like you want to be a good marketer more than you want to be good at finding
products.
But digressing here, coming back to the straight line, it's all the reason why people like they
boom, they, they like, they grow like crazy. They get to a couple hundred thousand dollars a
month. And then six months later, all of a sudden, like my ads aren't working.
Like my Facebook account sucks. Like what's going on? It's like, well, you just found a product,
which you're, which we're going to get into that had low sophistication. And so people weren't as
educated on it.

But nowadays, you know, what may have taken six years or at least, you know, three years now
is happening in three months or six months, right. As a result of like how fast information spreads
online. And so there now your product is been like, it's been exposed, right.
People have been aware to it, that market has started to get more sophisticated and they've
realized like either one, there's a better mechanism that you can go and get, which we will talk
about. And more importantly that, you know, your, your ads are just going to start to fail because
it's become more sophisticated. Other people are selling it.

FILE: AVATAR DEEP DIVE RESEARCH _1_ _1_.docx.txt
======================================================================

AVATAR DEEP DIVE RESEARCH
●​ Avatar Explanation: (A concise summary of your ideal customer —
who they are, what they’re going through, and what they’re looking for
in relation to their core problem.)​
●​ Age:​
●​ Gender:​
●​ Pain Points: (What specific problems or frustrations are causing
them the most emotional or physical discomfort?)​
●​ Day-to-Day Struggles: (What problems or frustrations do they face
daily because of their main issue?)​
●​ Victories: (What has worked for them in the past, even if just a little?
What small wins have they experienced?)​
●​ Failures: (What have they tried before that didn’t work? What past
attempts or solutions have failed them?)​
●​ Goals: (What are they trying to achieve in relation to their problem or
desired outcome?)​
●​ Beliefs: ( What do they currently believe about their problem,
possible solutions, or products like ours? )​
●​ Desires: ( What are their main and deeper desires? Start with “I want
to… so I can… so I can…” to uncover the true motivation behind
solving their problem)​

●​ Objections: ( What might prevent them from trying a solution or
choosing our product?)​
●​ Level of Awareness: (Refer to the customer's level of awareness as
defined in Breakthrough Advertising. )​
​
●​ Emotions & Identity: (What deeper motivations truly drive your
ideal customers? Go beyond surface-level answers like “confidence”
to uncover core desires like “authenticity” or “belonging.”)​
●​ Real Customer Language: (What exact words and phrases do they
use to describe their problem—and why? Understanding their
language is like having a cheat code for connection.)​
●​ Visual Cues: (What images or metaphors trigger action by
addressing their fear, doubt, or hesitation?)​
●​ Trigger Moments: (What situations or events push them to start
looking for a solution or make a purchase?)

A. Context:

When running Facebook ad tests for statics, I sometimes make bigger swings by testing different sub avatars within the same concept. In these cases, the creatives are intentionally speaking to different sub avatars or use cases.

Normally, I follow the 3-2-2 method with three creatives sharing two headlines and two primary texts. I’m unsure if that structure still makes sense when each creative is targeting a different sub avatar.

B. TLDR:

When testing multiple sub avatars in one concept,... See more

2

4 comments[Spencer Pawliw](https://www.skool.com/@spencer?g=origins)

🥷

• Jan 20

Keep the copies more generic when testing sub avatars

that said, if you are doing more native style ads where primary copy plays a big role, just focus on doing 2 copies for each individual

Since you have 3 different individual ads, you can optimize copies for each one, not the end of the world

1[Eman Akbarpour](https://www.skool.com/@eman-akbarpour-5114?g=origins)

• Jan 20

[@Spencer Pawliw](https://www.skool.com/@36c1d67731614624b0ba2be52ab97000?g=origins)

got it, thank you

0[Eman Akbarpour](https://www.skool.com/@eman-akbarpour-5114?g=origins)

• Jan 20

[@Spencer Pawliw](https://www.skool.com/@36c1d67731614624b0ba2be52ab97000?g=origins)

Also, when testing a Pubity style static ad, when structuring like a native ad without showing any product and just showing the problem with text at the bottom, should I still do a long form primary text or should I follow something like what is in the static at templates Canva dock with the regular/ shorter primary text?

0[Spencer Pawliw](https://www.skool.com/@spencer?g=origins)

Easy A Media
Belief

© Easy A Media Inc.

What Is Belief
The way your customers see reality
Your customers mental picture of the world they live in.

People believe in certain ways. These beliefs form a ﬁlter
through which your product must pass or be rejected.
Your ad MUST ﬁt in with your customers version of
“facts”

You leverage your CUSTOMERS logic, not your own, to
prove that your product works, to prove that people with
their belief system rely on your product, and prove no
other product satisﬁes his speciﬁc needs as well.

Credit to:
Eugene Schwartz
Nick Theriot

Easy A Media

Examples Of Belief
How beliefs make up our world

Easy A Media

Challenging Beliefs
How beliefs make up our world

If you violate your ideal customers established beliefs in
the slightest, then nothing you promise them, no matter
how appealing, can save your ad.

Easy A Media

Examples Of Belief
Making the promises they desire
