Helps you with gut health
Helps with wrinkles & aging
Helps with immune support
Helps with mood
Helps with stress
Helps improve memory
Helps improve …

Easy A Media

Making Too Many Promises
Making the promises they desire

Most brands try to strengthen ads by piling promise on
top of promise. Rather than, building believability and
strengthening one justiﬁable promise.

Easy A Media

The Importance of Belief
Building conviction in your customers

If you can channel belief, it can be more powerful than the strongest of desires.

The dominant Desire of your customers will determine the content for your ad
The Identity in which they deﬁne themselves will determine the visuals of your ad
The Beliefs of your customer will determine the development of your ad

The customer must have conviction that the desires & identity you are claiming/showing will in fact come true
through your product.

If your customer does not have BELIEF … they will NOT buy.

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #1 The “Yes” Train
Show the customer you’re talking about them, not someone else.

1.
2.
3.
4.
5.

Are you struggling to get to the gym every day?
Feel like you don’t have time to get a good workout in?
Tired of being judge or stared at in commercial gyms
Don’t want to spend $1,000’s on at-home equipment?
So you just give up on your ﬁtness goals entirely?

Then you’ve experienced what millions of others have
Which is why the BRRRN board allows you to take your ﬁtness
goals in a whole new direction.

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #1 The “Yes” Train
Show the customer you’re talking about them, not someone else.

Show the customer that you’re talking about him/her.
Not about someone else who would answer “No”.

Once the customer puts themselves in the “Yes” answer
group, then your recommendations will carry more
weight.

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #2 Symptoms
Detailed understanding of exactly what your customer is going through.
Every time I would put on my sock I debated whether or not to
even wear them.
That’s if they didn’t stink out the entire locker room ﬁrst, ofcourse.
The thin socks supposedly provide more grip
But all they ever do is rip.
And then as soon as you try thicker socks.
They suffocate your feet & you feel like you’re wearing clown shoes
on the ice.
Thats why Invented the Gameday Sock.
It’s was designed to give you the best of both worlds…🌎
A “sockless” barefoot feel in your skate.🦶
And more grip on the footbed of your skate. So you get more stride
power.💪
Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #2 Symptoms
Detailed understanding of exactly what your customer is going through.

Detail the symptoms or problems that are customers
reasons for NEEDING your product.

Prove that you have been there yourself, therefor your
recommendations will help answer their problems your shared problems.

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #3 Authority
I know you think this is true, but I’m going to show you why this is false
Forget everything you’ve ever heard or read about what age
“must-do” to your appearance.
Forget everything you have ever believed about how “old” you
look at thirty, forty, ﬁfty, or even sixty…
Because you are about to enter a new world of aging.
A world where scientiﬁc studies
$2,000,000 of my own money
and evidence that shows how aging can not only be slowed…
But reversed
-

Bryan Johnson

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #3 Authority
I know you think this is true, but I’m going to show you why this is false

Come out bluntly & Acknowledge the current beliefs,
but be prepared to show with strong authority the new
beliefs you wish to instill onto your customer.

You are not looking for agreement, you are loosening
previous beliefs to create new ones - that serve your
customer better.

Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #4 Logic
The most powerful words in building belief
An electronic hair dryer dries your hair by blowing heated air.
The hotter the air is coming out, the faster your hair will dry.
However hair molecules will be damaged if there is excessive heat.
So in order to to dry your hair fast without damaging your hair, you
must use the right amount of heat
And in order to use the right amount of heat, you must know the
right amount of heat to use
Because if you use too much, you will damage your hair
But if you use too little, it will take much longer to dry your hair
Which is why the Laifen automatically cycles the temperature
based on 100,000 of tests to ensure an optimal temperature
By auto rotating the temperature to keep it at the maximum
amount heat your hair can withstand
It while providing the fastest drying time. Without damaging your
hair.
Easy A Media

How Belief is Built - #4 Logic
The most powerful words in building belief

Prove your product works through bulletproof logical
thinking. One statement leading seamlessly into
another.

The reader has not only been told that it works, he has
been shown PROOF that it works.

Easy A Media

How To Create Belief
Painting the perfect picture
Actions:
Research, research, research.
What are the current beliefs of the market?
What are the current beliefs around your product?
What are the most common objections?
What are the claims that sound too good to be true?

customer has enough conviction to purchase your
product.

FILE: EAM _ 2025 - Market Awareness.txt
======================================================================

Easy A Media
Market Awareness

© Easy A Media Inc.

What Is Market Awareness
The level of understanding your customer has on your product

Credit to:
Eugene Schwartz
Nick Theriot

Easy A Media

Market Awareness - Most Aware
The levels of market awareness
Deﬁnition:
Most aware prospects already know what you do, they are just
price sensitive. All they need to do is make them an offer they
can’t resist
Goal:
It’s time to sell

Action:
Get out of the way, make a great offer, reduce risk & make it
easy for them to purchase.

Easy A Media

Market Awareness - Product Aware
The levels of market awareness
Deﬁnition:
The user is now aware of the options available and is exploring
them all, including yours. Your job is to now show them why
you’re the best.
Goal:
Make your product shine & show more of how it solves the
desire
Action:
Build more trust, share beneﬁts & features, show them more of
how your product will solve their desire the best. You can also
introduce future pacing at this level by showing them how to
use your solution & how easy, effective, and complete it is as
well.

Easy A Media

Market Awareness - Solution Aware
The levels of market awareness
Deﬁnition:
The user knows there are solutions to their problem, but has
not chosen one and does not know that your product will be
the best solution for them.
Goal:
Introduce your solution to the main problem as the superior
solution
Action:
Call out the beneﬁts consumers want that your product
provides. If there are other solutions that gives them the same
beneﬁts as yours, call out and compare how yours does it
better. You can also use success stories and inﬂuencer reviews
as vehicles for showing your reader just how well your solution
does that.

Easy A Media

Market Awareness - Problem Aware
The levels of market awareness
Deﬁnition:
The user senses they are experiencing a problem but doesn’t
know there is a solution.
Goal:
Identify and focus on the problem & show your understanding
of that problem. Then start to provide answers to that problem.
Action:
You must FIRST articulate the problem so clearly, that they say
“YES THAT’S THE PROBLEM I’M HAVING” and then start
introducing the solution to that problem.

Easy A Media

Market Awareness - Unaware
The levels of market awareness
Deﬁnition:
The user does not realize they have a problem.
Your market is identiﬁed by a core emotion they’re feeling. Call
out the core emotion, educate them on the problem that is
causing that emotion, then solution to solve that problem.
Goal:
Educate the unaware prospect to become problem aware.
Action:
You can either highlight a “problem” that you know everyone
has or is very common. Or talk about people “like” the ideal
prospect you’re wanting to go after.

Easy A Media

How Marketing Awareness Works Together
Where to enter the conversation & how to carry it forward

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication Lesson
What your consumers see
Lesson
These shirts represent what your potential customers see in the market.
When all of the shirts look the same, there is no clear or obvious choice… They all
seem to be the same, so you just pick one.
But when a well branded OR a new mechanism/product is introduced, it
automatically grabs attention and becomes the more attractive choice in the market

Easy A Media

What Is Market Sophistication
Understanding how many products & solutions have been
presented to that level of market awareness before

Market sophistication is the level of knowledge your
market has on your products/services.
How many similar products has your market been told
about before?
And how many similar products have they tried?

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication - Stage #1
State the claim
Deﬁnition:
First to market aka the Market Has Never Seen your product.
Goal:
Be Simple, Be Direct.

Action:
Name the claim & prove it works.

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication - Stage #2
Enlarge The Claim
Deﬁnition:
Market Is Starting To See It
Goal:
Enlarge the successful claim of competitors & one-up your
competitors.

Action:
Make bigger & stronger claims.

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication - Stage #3
Provide a New Mechanism (how to achieve the claim)
Deﬁnition:
Market Has Heard All The Claims

Goal:
Introduce a new mechanism

Action:
Focus on HOW the product delivers the SAME claim in a NEW &
better way

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication - Stage #4
Enlarge the Mechanism (Stage #2 applied to mechanism)
Deﬁnition:
Market Now Knows The Mechanisms

Goal:
Enlarging your mechanism.

Action:
Make it easier, faster, solve it better, overcome limitations.

Easy A Media

Market Sophistication - Stage #5
Identiﬁcation
Deﬁnition:
Market No Longer Believes You

Goal:
Focus your advertising on identiﬁcation with your prospect.

Action:
Show the market the version of themselves they want to
become, selling the persona of using your product.

Easy A Media
Desire

© Easy A Media Inc.

What Is Desire
The force that makes advertising work.
Desires are deﬁned as a strong feeling of wishing for something. A desire is related to
both needs and wants of people.
Desire is the demand that already exists and is ﬂowing through the market.
You CAN NOT create desire, you can only channel it.

Credit to:
Eugene Schwartz
Nick Theriot

Easy A Media

Types Of Desire In The Market
The force that makes advertising work.
There are 2 types of main desires that exist in the market
Permanent Desire - Health, Wealth, Relationships (Desires that never leave us)
Trending Desire - Crypto, Valentines Day, AI (Desires that come and go)

Easy A Media

Desire Strength
The force that makes advertising work.
The strength of your desire will determine the success of your ad.
Your goal is to ﬁnd the dominant desire based on:
Intensity → Minor headache VS severe migraine
Scope → The total amount of people that share the desire

Strong Desire

= Your Ad

Weak/No Desire

Easy A Media
Identiﬁcation

© Easy A Media Inc.

What Is Identiﬁcation
Painting the picture of who your customer desires to become
The desire of your customer to act out certain roles in their life.
How your customer deﬁnes themselves to the world as a speciﬁc kind of person.
It is the desire, not for satisfaction, but for recognition & expression

Identity marketing is about showcasing WHO your product
helps your customer become.

Identiﬁcation marketing is how we communicate
the feelings our customer desires, without words

Credit to:
Eugene Schwartz
Nick Theriot

Easy A Media

How Identiﬁcation is Built
Symbols of identity

Easy A Media

How Identiﬁcation is Built
Symbols of identity

Easy A Media

Examples Of Identiﬁcation
Identiﬁcation in ads
We all have an image of ourselves, or a sense of who we are; this is
called our self-image. We don’t just buy objects, we buy roles.
Your ability to identify who your potential customer is and leverage
that in your marketing unspokenly will determine your success.

A woman buys low calorie food to become thinner, but in so doing
she also becomes a more attractive, youthful looking women
without having to say it.
A Man buys a car for power, speed, and transportation, equally as
much as for the projection of prestige, success, and wealth that
communicates without words.

Easy A Media

Identiﬁcation In Content
How identiﬁcation creates success
Technique used by 9 ﬁgure brands

Easy A Media

Identiﬁcation In Content
How identiﬁcation creates success
Technique used by our brands

Identity #1
Identity #2

Easy A Media

Identiﬁcation In Marketing
How identiﬁcation inﬂuences decisions

Nescafé discovered this early on in the launch of its ﬁrst instant coffee in the 1950s. The product was initially
positioned as a time-saver; no longer would you have to spend 10 minutes brewing a pot of coffee.
It did not catch on. The reason, it turned out, was that saving time and effort in this way clashed with the
identity of most women, who during that decade felt pressured to be perfect homemakers for hardworking
husbands.
So the company changed its approach. Instead of claiming that the new product would reduce the time it
took to make your husband coffee, the ads suggested it would help you serve him better coffee and leave
you extra time to do more for him.
Dated and offensive as this may sound today, the reframing was effective. Sales tripled during the decade
and had increased 12-fold by the mid-1970s.

Easy A Media

Identiﬁcation In Marketing
How identiﬁcation inﬂuences decisions
Identiﬁcation got me to spend $30,000+

“agency owner”

Easy A Media

How To Find Identiﬁcation
Painting the perfect picture
Ask Yourself:
What is the role your product offers to your prospect?
How do I associate & turn my product into an “instrument” for my customer
to use in order to achieve that role?

Actions:
Research, research, research.
Where does my ideal customer want to be? (environment to show in the visual)
What does my ideal customer want to look like? (The model)
What does my ideal customer want to feel? (the story of the ad)
What self image do my customers want to portray? (The expression)

FILE: Evolve - Desire Overview.txt
======================================================================

Why you need to understand Desire
before creating an Avatar?
The Foundation: What Are Desires?
Mass Desire = "The public spread of a private want"
When millions of people privately want the same thing, it becomes a mass desire that
advertising can harness.
Why Desires are important for building Avatars:
A 22-year-old and 45-year-old can share the same desire (make more money, find love, etc.).
So, it’s about understanding what a group of people WANT before narrowing down the avatar.
The larger the desire, the more specific you can go on the avatar.
Key Rule: You cannot CREATE desires. You can only CHANNEL existing desires onto your
product.

How Desires Are Born: The 2 Forces (Spencer’s Edition)
1. Permanent Forces - The Things People Will Always Have
●​ Mass Instincts: Work on anyone, anywhere, anytime. Caveman or CEO.
What every human wants (and will always continue to want)
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​
●​

Health - Survival, Stay alive, stay healthy, and safe
Sex - Be attractive, find a mate, feel desired
Status - Be important, respected, better than others
Belonging - Fit in, have friends, not be rejected
Control - Have power, predict what happens, be in charge
Comfort - Make life easier, avoid pain and work

Survival (Health) drive existed 10,000 years ago, exists today, will exist in 10,000 more years.
Status drive existed 10,000 years ago, exists today, will exist in 10,000 years.
The way we satiate these instinctual desires changes, but the desires will always be there

2. Permanent Forces - The Things People Will Always Have
●​ Mass Technological Problems: No matter the year, technology will cause problems for
people. Whether people have direct experience or indirect awareness of the problem.
What technology keeps screwing up:
●​ 1. Complexity - Too hard to figure out how it works. ​
The buttons are confusing. The instructions don't make sense. ​
You feel dumb.​

🧠

●​ 2. Overwhelm - Too many choices, beeps, and distractions​
Your phone won't stop buzzing. You have 47 apps. There are too many streaming
services. ​
You feel anxious.​

🧠

●​ 3. Fragility - Breaks right when you need it most ​
Your phone dies during an important call. Your WiFi crashes during a big meeting.
You feel frustrated.​

🧠

●​ 4. Maintenance - Always needs fixing, updating, or paying for​
Your apps want updates every day. Your subscription costs keep going up. Something
always needs to be fixed or renewed. ​
You feel tired​

🧠

●​ 5. Incompatibility - Won't work with your other stuff ​
Your old iPhone charger doesn't work with your new one. Nothing connects easily.​
You feel trapped​

🧠

●​ 6. Obsolescence - Gets old and useless fast ​
Your phone becomes "too old" for new apps. Last year's gadget is already outdated.​
You feel cheated.

🧠

TV reception was terrible in the 1960s, internet connection is shaky today, and holograms will
lag in 2050.
The technology changes, but the problems persist.

3. Forces of Change - What Changes How the Permanent Desires are
Fulfilled
●​ Style Trends: What becomes fashionable (swimming pools vs. bigger cars)
●​ Mass Education: What society learns (health awareness, new Inventions)

Examples Of The Process

🐶DOG HARNESS:

Example 1 - Mass Instinct + Style Trend
●​ Mass Instinct: Status (not being embarrassed)
●​ Current Force: Style Trend - "Dogs are family members" culture
●​ Channel: "Be the dog parent everyone admires"
Example 2 - Mass Instinct + Mass Education:
●​ Mass Instinct: Health (keep dog safe)
●​ Mass Education: People learned that collar pulling damages the trachea
●​
Channel: "Why are you still choking your dog in 2025?"

⭐️

Example 3 - Mass Tech Problem + Style Trend:
●​ Mass Tech Problem: Fragility
●​ Style Trend: Only buying products that are "built-to-last"
●​ Channel: "The last harness you'll ever buy"
Example 4 - Mass Tech Problem + Mass Education
●​ Mass Tech Problem: Incompatibility (won't work with different dog body types)
●​ Mass Education: People learned that dogs can slip out of regular harnesses
●​ Channel: "The harness Houdini can't escape"

🪟 BLACKOUT BLINDS:
Example 1 - Mass Instinct + Style Trend
●​ Mass Instinct: Comfort (avoiding discomfort/bad sleep)
●​ Style Trend: "Locking in" is cool (commitment/focus culture)
●​ Channel: "Time to lock in perfect sleep"

Example 2: Mass Instinct + Mass Education
●​ Mass Instinct: Health (staying healthy)
●​ Mass Education: People learned light disrupts sleep cycles (poor sleep = health
problems)
●​ Channel: "Light is slowly killing you."
Example 3: Mass Tech Problem + Mass Education
●​ Mass Tech Problem: Complexity (too hard to install)
●​ Mass Education: People learned that installation usually sucks
●​ Channel: "No tools. No drilling. No BS."
Example 4 - Mass Tech Problem + Style Trend
●​ Mass Tech Problem: Complexity (sleep optimization is complicated)
●​ Style Trend: Biohacking/sleep optimization is trendy
●​ Channel: "Biohack your bedroom in 5 minutes"

How to Find & Evaluate Desires
Step 1: Spot the Desire
●​
●​
●​
●​

Watch for complaint patterns
Look for “wants” and “needs”
Follow cultural trends
Look at where people spend money

Step 2: Test Its Power (The 3 Dimensions)
Scope: How many people share this desire? (fine antiques VS making money)​
Urgency: How desperately do they want/need relief? (broken bone VS cut)​
Staying Power: Does this desire renew continuously? (hunger VS Going to paris)

Step 3: Choose the Strongest
Pick the desire with the highest combination of all three dimensions. That said Here is the power
rankings based on the instincts Above:​
​
#1 - Health - Still ultimate. Rich or poor, health scares move everyone​
#2 - Status - Amplified massively by social media. More visible competition than ever​
#3 - Sex - Fundamental but more subtle in modern marketing (regulations/platforms)​
#4 - Comfort - Elevated because technology made it possible + expected​

#5 - Control - Boosted by modern complexity. People want control over chaotic systems​
#6 - Belonging - Important but less survival-urgent (don't need tribe to literally survive)

The Elevation Strategy
Moving products within the hierarchy can lead to more scale
However, moving products down can lower scale.
If you try to take a luxury watch (Status) and position it as "convenient timekeeping" (Comfort), it
feels cheap and wrong. You're reducing its perceived value.
Moving down = less emotional urgency = lower prices = weaker positioning = competing in
commodity categories instead of premium markets.
Key Rule: The elevation must feel authentic. Force it, and customers sense the manipulation.

🐶DOG HARNESS:

Level 6: Belonging (Weakest)
●​ "Join the responsible dog owner community."
●​ "Every caring dog owner needs this."
Level 5: Comfort (Weak)
●​ "No more struggling with your dog"
●​ "Finally, stress-free dog walks"
Level 4: Control (Moderate)
●​ "Take control of every walk"
●​ "Master your dog's pulling instantly"
Level 3: Sex (Strong)
●​ "She'll ask where you got that harness"
●​ "Turn heads on every walk"
Level 2: Status (Very Strong)
●​ "Why celebrity trainers won't use anything else"

●​ "The harness only 3% of dog owners know about"
Level 1: Health (Strongest)
●​ "Why vets are begging owners to switch"
●​ "Why are you still choking your dog in 2025?"

How Desires Change Throughout The Year:
Evolve - The Desire Calendar

Final Words From Schwartz's + Spencer
Never Fight Desires: You will fail if you go after nonexistent desires.
Education Is Powerful: Successful companies can educate audiences through their ads (more
on this later)
Timing is Important: The Same product can succeed or fail based on which desires are
rising/falling and what mass education or trends are happening
Analysis Paralysis: Don't write like a mechanical robot. What's important is to be able to
identify desires from headlines & going deeper on understanding why specific ones are working.
Execution Above All: If you've done the research and you know the desire exists, you need to
understand your execution (how you communicate the desire) is what will lead to real success

FILE: Evolve - The Desire Calendar.txt
======================================================================

The Evolve Desire Calendar ​

How Desires Change Throughout The Year:
The Evidence-Based Desire Calendar
Based on comprehensive analysis of US consumer behavior data from 2021-2025

1️⃣ JANUARY: Transformation Desires Peak
TOP 3 CORE DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ CONTROL - January triggers the strongest "Fresh Start Effect" with 47% spike in gym
visits and massive organization/decluttering behavior as people seek control after
holiday chaos.​
2.​ HEALTH - Winter's harshest months trigger ancestral conservation instincts, driving
35-40% increase in health spending (vitamins, supplements, preventive care).​
3.​ STATUS - Career advancement and self-improvement peak as people seek to become
"better versions" of themselves.
Surface Desires:
●​ Physical Transformation: "New year, new me" mentality peaks → HEALTH
(health/staying alive)​
●​ Organization & Control: Home organization, decluttering surge → CONTROL (having
power over environment)​
●​ Financial Discipline: Budgeting, saving, "getting my finances together" → CONTROL
(having power over finances)​
●​ Career Advancement: Job searching, skill development plans → STATUS (being
respected, better than others)
Opportunity: Target transformation desires when motivation is highest, but keep price points
accessible as consumers recover from holiday spending.

The Data:
●​
●​
●​

12% of all gym memberships start in January, with a 25-30% increase in enrollment driven by New Year's resolutions
Consumer spending intentions down across discretionary categories as people revert to typical post-holiday restraint
Fitness and health retailers see peak demand, while luxury retailers experience post-holiday spending fatigue

2️⃣ FEBRUARY: Relationship & Self-Love Desires

💕

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ SEX - Valentine's Day drives $27.5 billion in spending as hormonal surge creates 30%
increase in dating app activity and appearance-focused purchases.
2.​ BELONGING - Record 32% spending on friends and 19% on co-workers shows
expansion beyond romantic love to all social connections.
3.​ STATUS - Social recognition through thoughtful gift-giving peaks as people want to be
seen as caring and generous.
Core Desires:
●​ Romantic Connection: Traditional Valentine's focus on significant others → SEX (be
attractive, find mate, feel desired)
●​ Platonic Love: Record spending on friends, family, co-workers → BELONGING (fit in,
have friends, not be rejected)
●​ Self-Love: "Galentine's Day" and treating yourself trends → SEX (feel attractive/desired)
●​ Social Recognition: Being seen as thoughtful and caring → STATUS (be respected,
better than others)
Opportunity: Expand beyond romantic love to all forms of appreciation and self-care.
The Data:
●​
●​

Valentine's Day spending reached record $27.5 billion in 2025, up from $25.8 billion in 2024
56% of consumers plan to celebrate Valentine's Day, with average spending of $188.81 per person

●​

32% plan to purchase gifts for friends (highest in survey history), 19% for co-workers (also a record)

3️⃣ + 4️⃣ MARCH-APRIL: Action & Renewal Desires

🌱

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ CONTROL - Spring cleaning and home renewal surge as increasing daylight boosts
serotonin and drives "Fresh Start Effect" behaviors.
2.​ HEALTH - Physical revival and outdoor exercise motivation return as vitamin D levels
rise and winter health concerns peak.
3.​ BELONGING - Social reconnection intensifies with spring break travel ($8,306 average)
and gathering planning as people emerge from winter isolation.
Core Desires:
●​ Home Renewal: Spring cleaning, refreshing living spaces → CONTROL (having power
over environment)
●​ Physical Revival: Getting back outdoors, exercise motivation returns → HEALTH
(staying healthy and alive)
●​ Social Reconnection: Planning gatherings, travel, experiences → BELONGING (have
friends, not be rejected)
●​ Fresh Start Energy: Second wave of motivation → CONTROL (having power over what
happens)
Opportunity: "Spring forward" messaging resonates as people emerge from winter hibernation
mode.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

McKinsey data shows consumers across age groups increase spending on home improvement and gardening supplies as
they prepare for spring
Garden centers and DIY stores see major upticks, while winter gear sales decline
35% of Americans plan spring break vacations, with average costs reaching $8,306 in 2025

5️⃣ MAY: Appreciation & Memorial Desires
TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ BELONGING - Mother's Day ($34.1B) and Memorial Day drive family connection and
multi-generational bonding as spring social activities peak.
2.​ STATUS - Gratitude expression and honoring maternal figures shows respectfulness
and social awareness, key status behaviors.
3.​ COMFORT - Outdoor living preparation (BBQ equipment, patio furniture) as people
prepare for summer entertaining and relaxation.
Core Desires:

●​ Gratitude Expression: Honoring mothers and maternal figures → STATUS (being
respected, seen as thoughtful)
●​ Family Connection: Multi-generational celebration and appreciation → BELONGING
(fit in with family, not be rejected)
●​ Outdoor Living: Preparing for summer entertaining and relaxation → COMFORT (make
life easier, avoid discomfort)
●​ Memorial Reflection: Honoring sacrifice while celebrating freedom → BELONGING
(participating in cultural traditions)
Opportunity: Focus on appreciation, family bonds, and preparing for summer lifestyle.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Mother's Day spending expected to reach $34.1 billion in 2025, with average spending of $259.04 per person
Memorial Day weekend sees record 45.1 million Americans traveling, marking start of summer travel season
Outdoor furniture and BBQ equipment sellers see major sales increases

6️⃣ JUNE: Achievement & Paternal Desires
TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ STATUS - Graduation season and Father's Day ($22.4B) create peak achievement
recognition and celebration of accomplishments.
2.​ COMFORT - Summer vacation planning intensifies (70% plan vacations, $3,471
average) as people seek freedom and enjoyable experiences.
3.​ BELONGING - Family celebrations and life transitions create strong bonding and
support-seeking behaviors.
Core Desires:
●​ Achievement Recognition: Celebrating graduations, accomplishments → STATUS
(being respected, celebrated)
●​ Paternal Appreciation: Father's Day focus on tools, tech, experiences → STATUS
(showing respect) + BELONGING (family bonds)
●​ Freedom & Adventure: Summer vacation planning intensifies → COMFORT (make life
easier, more enjoyable)
●​ Life Transitions: Supporting graduates, new chapters → STATUS (celebrating
achievements)
Opportunity: Combine achievement celebration with summer preparation messaging.
The Data:

●​
●​
●​

Father's Day spending expected to reach $22.4 billion in 2024, second-highest in survey history
Graduation season drives demand for gift cards, tech products, and celebratory items
70% of Americans plan summer vacations, with average trip budgets of $3,471

7️⃣ + 8️⃣ JULY-AUGUST: Lifestyle & Preparation Desires

☀️

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ COMFORT - Peak summer lifestyle with 3.1 average trips per person and immediate
gratification seeking as vitamin D and dopamine reach annual highs.
2.​ STATUS - Social status through summer activities peaks as body image concerns and
"looking good" for vacations dominate spending.
3.​ CONTROL - Back-to-school preparation anxiety ($128B spending) drives early planning
and organization behaviors.
Core Desires:
●​ Present-Moment Living: Summer experiences, immediate gratification → COMFORT
(avoid pain, enjoy pleasure)
●​ Social Status: Looking good for summer activities and vacations → STATUS (be better
than others)
●​ Preparation Anxiety: Getting ready for back-to-school, fall routines → CONTROL
(having power over future)
●​ Family Harmony: Creating memories while managing back-to-school stress →
BELONGING (maintaining relationships)
Opportunity: Balance "enjoy now" summer messaging with "prepare for fall" planning.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Swimwear and beach accessories dominate, while school supply shopping begins early
67% of back-to-school shoppers start purchasing by early July, with total spending expected to reach $128 billion in 2025
Americans take average of 3.1 summer trips, with 41% planning trips of 3 nights or fewer

9️⃣ SEPTEMBER: Achievement & Structure Desires

📚

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ CONTROL - Back-to-school creates "September is the new January" fresh start effect
with structure and routine re-establishment.
2.​ STATUS - Learning, growth, and ensuring children's success drive $127.6B in combined
spending as parents seek social approval.
3.​ BELONGING - "Back-to-school energy" affects all adults, creating community
participation and social integration behaviors.
Core Desires:

●​ Structure & Routine: Re-establishing school year rhythms → CONTROL (having power
over environment)
●​ Learning & Growth: Education, skill-building, productivity focus → STATUS (being
better, more respected)
●​ Fresh Start: "September is the new January" mentality → CONTROL (having power
over what happens)
●​ Parental Success: Ensuring children are prepared and successful → STATUS (being
seen as good parent)
Opportunity: Lead with CONTROL ("Take charge of your fall routine") then layer STATUS ("Be
the parent/professional everyone admires"). September's $127.6B spending proves people will
pay premium for structure and success during this secondary fresh start period.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Back-to-school shoppers complete their $38.8 billion in spending, second-highest on record
Back-to-college spending hits $88.8 billion, with 43% saying they need more new items than last year
Coffee and tea sellers see increased sales as temperatures cool

🔟 OCTOBER: Identity & Seasonal Desires 🎃
TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ STATUS - Halloween costume choices ($3.8B) reveal aspirational identities and
"Maskenfreiheit" - freedom to explore who you want to be.
2.​ BELONGING - Cultural tradition participation peaks with 77% buying decorations as
"cuffing season" begins and social bonding intensifies.
3.​ CONTROL - Seasonal transformation through home decorating allows environmental
control as daylight decreases trigger nesting behaviors.
Core Desires:
●​ Identity Expression: Costume choices reveal aspirational selves → STATUS (showing
who you are, being respected)
●​ Social Belonging: Participating in cultural traditions and parties → BELONGING
(participating in traditions)
●​ Seasonal Transformation: Home and yard decorating as seasonal identity →
CONTROL (power over environment)
●​ Controlled Fear: Safe scares and controlled excitement → COMFORT (safe
excitement, avoiding real danger)
Opportunity: Halloween has become "a whole season" - start messaging in September.

The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Halloween spending reached $12.2 billion in 2023, $11.6 billion in 2024
77% purchase decorations ($3.8B), 69% buy costumes ($3.8B), with 47% starting shopping before October
Halloween decorations spending increased 42% from 2019-2024 ($2.6B to $3.8B)

1️⃣1⃣️NOVEMBER: Gratitude & Preparation Desires

🦃

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ BELONGING - Thanksgiving drives family harmony and tradition participation as
seasonal affective patterns increase bonding needs.
2.​ COMFORT - Black Friday deal-hunting ($10.8B online) provides dopamine surges from
"winning" while avoiding financial pain.
3.​ CONTROL - Holiday preparation intensifies (45% begin shopping) as people seek to
avoid December stress and chaos.
Core Desires:
●​ Family Harmony: Thanksgiving gathering success and tradition → BELONGING (fit in
with family)
●​ Value & Savings: Black Friday deal-hunting intensifies → COMFORT (make life easier,
avoid financial pain)
●​ Gifting Preparation: Early holiday shopping to avoid stress → CONTROL (having
power over holiday success)
●​ Gratitude Expression: Giving thanks and counting blessings → STATUS (being seen
as thoughtful)
Opportunity: Bridge gratitude messaging with gift preparation and value positioning.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Black Friday online sales hit $10.8 billion in 2024 (10.2% increase), with 200 million shoppers participating
Thanksgiving drives demand for roasts, side dishes, festive décor
73% plan to shop Black Friday, with 45% beginning holiday shopping this month

1️⃣2⃣️DECEMBER: Connection & Celebration Desires

🎄

TOP 3 PRIORITY DESIRES & WHY:
1.​ BELONGING - Peak emotional spending ($240.8B) prioritizes relationships over price as
winter's low serotonin intensifies connection needs.
2.​ COMFORT - Holiday traditions and celebrations ($261 average on seasonal products)
provide warmth and joy during year's darkest months.

3.​ STATUS - Gift-giving ($641 average) demonstrates care and social awareness while
maintaining relationship hierarchies and obligations.
Core Desires:
●​ Connection & Love: Expressing care through gift-giving → BELONGING (not be
rejected, maintain relationships)
●​ Tradition & Continuity: Maintaining family and cultural traditions → BELONGING
(participating in cultural celebrations)
●​ Joy & Celebration: Creating magical moments and memories → COMFORT (avoid
pain, enjoy pleasure)
●​ Social Belonging: Participating in cultural celebrations → BELONGING (participating in
celebrations)
Opportunity: Peak emotional spending when people prioritize relationships over price.
The Data:
●​
●​
●​

Cyber Monday reached record $13.3 billion in 2024, with total holiday season spending hitting $240.8 billion
Average holiday spending per person reached record $902 in 2024, with $641 on gifts and $261 on seasonal products
Toy and game retailers, jewelry stores, and gourmet food sellers dominate

The Data-Driven Insights
Spending Intensity Rankings (by month):
1.​ December: $240.8B total holiday spending
2.​ August-September: $128B back-to-school/college spending
3.​ May: $34.1B Mother's Day spending
4.​ February: $27.5B Valentine's Day spending
5.​ June: $22.4B Father's Day spending
6.​ October: $11.6B Halloween spending
Key Behavioral Patterns:
●​ 45% of consumers start holiday shopping on Black Friday, with 33% starting on Cyber
Monday
●​ 67% start back-to-school shopping in July, 2 months early
●​ 47% start Halloween shopping before October (up from 32% in 2014)
Sources: National Retail Federation annual surveys, McKinsey ConsumerWise data, Bureau of
Labor Statistics, fitness industry reports, retail seasonality studies

FILE: Evolve Avatar - Reflection Modules.txt
======================================================================

The New Overview of the “How To Prepare For Ads” section
In this module, I'm going to help you understand why we go through the things that we do in this
upcoming series so you can TRULY understand the strategy behind Origins.
A lot of courses tell you what to do
Some courses show you or tell you how to do it.
But very few actually explain WHY you should do it → Other than the obvious “so you can
make more money”
So, my goal with this module is to help you understand the WHY behind every step in this
section.
So…
WHY DOES THIS SECTION MATTER?
Because the REAL secret behind winning ads that most people don’t understand
Is the SHITLOAD of work winning ads require prior to making them
AKA your preparation.
The effort you put in before making ads is the difference between
Making ads that scale and making ads that don’t.
So…
Before you start to just “make ads”
You have to understand WHAT makes ads work
And in order to understand WHAT makes them work
You need to go deep on marketing fundamentals and learn the psychology behind why people
buy
And that first begins with product

1.​ Why do you need to understand your product?
Well, it's pretty hard to solve someone's problem or provide value to someone if you don't
actually understand what value your product provides.
So going deeper on your product, allows you to know what value you can provide to others
All your trying to do is convince someone that your product provides the same value (ideally
more) than what you charge.

2.​ Why do you need to understand desire?
Desire is simply what people WANT.
People buy things out of “wants” and “needs”
So if your going to convince someone to buy, you need to understand at a deep level WHY that
person wants to buy
That “deeper why” is their desire
If your stuck on desires, use the “Why Train”
I want to get a new shirt → Why? → Because I want to improve my style → Why? → Because I
want to look more attractive → Why? → Because I want to be in a relationship and fall in love
(Now we are getting somewhere)
^We use this information to help guide our ads.

3.​ Why do you need to understand market sophistication?
As much as you know your product can provide value, and as much as you know that there are
specific people who have desires that you can help
Most people will NOT believe you
OR
Have already tried your “thing” to fulfill their desire

The best way to explain market sophistication
Imagine you see an ad on Facebook that says, "I will make you a millionaire in ecom”
Are you going to believe that person?
Probably not, because you've heard other people make that claim.
That is market sophistication
And that is exactly how your customers feel
So if you don’t understand how to “cut through” that sophistication that exists in the market in
order to build BELIEF
What your product does, and your customers desires, are irrelevant.

4.​ Why do you need to know about new mechanisms, new information, and avatars?
Because you aren’t good at writing copy
Which makes sense.
You’re a beginner.
I was terrible at writing copy for years.
Now that you are aware of that, you can push yourself to get better at writing
But more importantly you can focus on those 3 ways (that all work together) to build belief in
your ad
New Mechanisms = build belief
New Information = build belief
New Identities* (avatars) = build belief
Instead of being “really good with words” → AKA really good at copywriting
Just use your customers words to write more relatable copy
And if your copy is relatable = good copy

So it’s a way to “write good copy” without actually being “good at writing copy”

5.​ Basic research processes still apply.
Reddit → You see me use this a lot
YouTube → Look up your customer pain points → “how to fix X” “How to get rid of Y” etc.
Google → Look up solutions to your customers problems → What do they see?
Amazon → Read reviews on similar products/solutions and identify the problems
TikTok → What videos are being made about the product? Read the comments
Competitors → You guys already know this, but what are those reviews saying about the
product?
etc.

Market Desires

17:25

---

**Slides:**  
[https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Mv-wr2ePnnI\_00myK11WHUXCj9bLZuKbohk9s83fwc0/edit?usp=sharing](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Mv-wr2ePnnI_00myK11WHUXCj9bLZuKbohk9s83fwc0/edit?usp=sharing "https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Mv-wr2ePnnI_00myK11WHUXCj9bLZuKbohk9s83fwc0/edit?usp=sharing")

## Understanding Desires:

**Mass Desire** = "The public spread of a private want"

When millions of people privately want the same thing, it becomes a **mass desire** that advertising can harness.

**Key Rule**: You cannot CREATE desires. You can only CHANNEL existing desires onto your product.

---

## How Desires Are Born: The Two Forces

### Permanent Forces (Always There)

- **Mass Instincts**: Universal human drives (health, attractiveness, security)
- **Technological Problems**: Ongoing issues (car repairs, slow internet)
- These create **stable, recurring desires** that never go away

### Forces of Change (Create New Desires)

- **Style Trends**: What becomes fashionable (swimming pools vs. bigger cars)
- **Mass Education**: What society learns (health awareness, new technologies)
- **Cultural Shifts**: Changes in values (women's liberation, environmental consciousness)
- These create **emerging desires** and shift existing ones

---

## The Three Desire Categories

Once a desire exists (from the forces above), it falls into one of these types:

**Physical Desires**

- Health, strength, attractiveness, freedom from pain, sensory satisfaction

**Material Desires**

- Money, possessions, status symbols, security, convenience

**Emotional/Social Desires**

- Love, acceptance, recognition, success, power, adventure

---

## How to Find & Evaluate Desires

### Step 1: Spot the Desire

- Watch for complaint patterns
- Study successful competitors
- Follow cultural trends
- Look where people spend money

### Step 2: Test Its Power (The 3 Dimensions)

**Urgency**: How desperately do they want relief? **Staying Power**: Does this desire renew continuously? **Scope**: How many people share this desire?

### Step 3: Choose the Strongest

Pick the desire with the highest combination of all three dimensions.

---

## The Process in Action

**1\. Forces Create Desire** Example: Social media culture (Force of Change) → People want to look "Instagram-worthy" (New Mass Desire)

**2\. Categorize the Desire**  
"Instagram-worthy" = Physical desire (appearance) + Emotional desire (social recognition)

**3\. Channel onto Your Product** Beauty brand: "Get that perfect selfie glow with our new foundation"

---

## Schwartz's Key Insights

**Never Fight Desires**: The Edsel failed because it fought the mass desire for compact cars

**Ride the Wave**: Successful companies spot desires early and channel them onto their products

**Timing is Everything**: Same product can succeed or fail based on which desires are rising/falling

**Specificity Wins**: Don't just say "people want to be attractive" - find the specific way they want to be attractive right now

---

## Your Job as a Marketer

1. **Be a Desire Detective**: Constantly watch for emerging desires and shifting patterns
2. **Pick Your Lane**: Choose one powerful desire to focus on
3. **Channel, Don't Create**: Direct existing desires onto your product
4. **Stay Alert**: Desires change - be ready to pivot when they do

**The Bottom Line**: Master this, and you can predict what people will want to buy before your competitors even know there's a market for it.

<audio><source type="audio/mpeg"></audio>

<audio><source type="audio/mpeg"></audio>

FILE: Market Sophistication Document.txt
======================================================================

Why you need to understand Market
Sophistication before creating an Avatar.
Eugene Schwartz's Market Sophistication Theory
Eugene Schwartz's Market Sophistication from "Breakthrough Advertising" in 1966 remains
relevant today.
However, the rate at which these markets sophisticate is now faster than ever.
50 years ago, it would take word of mouth to reach others around whether or not a product
actually worked well in their local areas.
But also, keep in mind that global commerce was not the same 50 years ago as it is today.
So, as the scale of selling has increased
So has the scale of customer feedback & word of mouth.

The five stages of market sophisitcaiton
Markets progress through five stages based on customer exposure to competitive messaging
and product claims.
Stage 1 - First to Market: The pioneering phase where simple benefit statements suffice.
Customers haven't encountered the product category before, making direct claims effective.
Stage 2 - Competition Emerges: Multiple players enter, forcing benefit amplification. Brands
compete through enhanced promises and superlatives.

⭐️ Stage 3 - Market Saturation: Customers become skeptical after hearing repetitive

promises. Success requires introducing new mechanisms explaining HOW products work
differently.

⭐️ Stage 4 - Mechanism Competition: Multiple brands claim unique mechanisms, forcing

enhancement and elaboration. Brands must make their mechanisms "easier, faster, surer" than
competitors

⭐️ Stage 5 - Complete Sophistication: Customers actively resist all claims, seeking reasons
NOT to buy. Only emotional connection, identity, and brand experience drive decisions.

Market Evolution in Sophisticated eCom Markets
eCommerce has changed from simple product sales to extremely sophisticated psychological
positioning battles.
Which is why most people say:
“eCommerce is too saturated”
“eCommerce is too hard”
(Skill Issue)
Nowadays more than ¾ of potential consumers research products before purchase, with the
average eCommerce category containing 15+ direct competitors.
Traditional "bigger, better, faster" claims no longer resonate with customers who have "heard it
all before."
Success requires understanding your market's sophistication level and deploying appropriate
strategic responses.

Why Stages 3-5 Dominate Modern eCommerce
Most eCommerce markets operate in Stages 3-5 due to the internet lowering entry barriers
and the overall abundance of information online
Consumers are more educated than ever.
Reports and studies actually show that 67% of purchase decisions are now influenced by brand
story and values rather than features.
This confirms Stage 4-5 characteristics across big categories.

🚨As a result, you get something called the Avatar-Market Sophistication Gap 🚨
Meaning, even though your:
-​
-​
-​

Avatar is perfectly defined.
The desires are crystal clear
And your product actually delivers your claims.

You fail to communicate to those avatars in a way that breaks through the sophistication level.
And as a result, your ads don’t work.

Important Things To Note About Market Sophistcation:
1.​ This is what makes Facebook advertising extremely hard when you have no true USPs​
2.​ This is the reason why bad marketers can get rich quickly when they find a product with
a New Mechanism or in early stage 1-2 markets​
3.​

⭐️This is not something you test on your ads... You don’t test “different levels of
sophistication” Your sophistication just exists​

4.​ Market Sophistication is NOT Market Awareness.

Three Strategic Responses for Sophisticated Markets

⚙️New Mechanism: Revolutionary delivery and technology
The New Mechanism strategy introduces a NEW processes, technology, or delivery system that
makes familiar promises fresh and believable.
Rather than claiming bigger benefits, you reveal the "secret thing" behind your solution.
Psychological foundation: Sophisticated & educated customers crave a CLEAR
understanding of HOW products work. (however this can be over complicated - more on this
later)
Examples of Great New Mechanisms:
●​ Purple's GelFlex Grid departed entirely from traditional foam with visible, demonstrable
properties
●​ Seed Health's ViaCap® dual-capsule system ensures 95% probiotic survival versus
competitors' 20%
●​ Oura Ring: Developed a NEW way for users to acheive their fitness & health goals.

The biggest mistakes people make with this are over-technical communication that alienates
audiences, claiming false uniqueness for industry-standard processes, and explaining
mechanisms without connecting to customer value.
The Apple Ipod Story:
South Korean company first created the MP3
But only until Apple marketed the MP3 as “1,000 songs in your pocket” did people start buying
it.

💡New Information: Education as competitive advantage

The New Information strategy reframes product understanding through consumer education,
positioning brands as trusted authorities rather than sellers.
This approach helps reveals problems customers didn't know existed while providing solutions.
Psychological foundation: Education builds authority and trust with skeptical audiences.
The reciprocity principle means customers feel obligated to support brands that provide genuine
value.
Problem awareness creation helps sophisticated buyers recognize unmet needs they hadn't
articulated.
Market-leading examples:
●​ Athletic Greens (AG1) invested $5+ million in clinical studies, created comprehensive
educational content about 75 ingredients, and built a $600+ million business through
science-first positioning.​
●​ The Ordinary disrupted luxury skincare through radical ingredient transparency, naming
products after active ingredients with concentrations​
●​ Ritual vitamins provides complete supply chain transparency with interactive ingredient
maps showing exact source locations
The information strategy requires real expertise on the niche.​
​
Surface-level education or overly promotional content undermines credibility.
Remember, your potential customers are educated. But you can still be an expert.

👤New Identity: Lifestyle movements and tribal belonging

The New Identity strategy creates emotional connections through lifestyle movements, focusing
on WHO customers become rather than what products do.
This approach builds communities around shared values and aspirations.
It also massively INFLUENCES status (how other perceive them)
Psychological foundation: People make purchases to maintain identity consistency.
Maslow's hierarchy shows belonging as a core human need that brands can fulfill.
Emotional responses to advertising have 23% greater influence on purchase intent than rational
content.
Breakthrough implementations:
●​ Liquid Death created a $1.4 billion water brand through punk rock identity and
environmental activism​
●​ Glossier built a beauty empire around "skin first, makeup second" philosophy and
authentic self-expression​
●​ Peloton transformed exercise equipment into status symbols with celebrity instructors
and competitive communities
Identity strategies fail when positioning feels inauthentic, values misalign with actual brand
behavior, or attempts to appeal to everyone result in meaningless positioning.

3️⃣ The Big 3 Recapped
1. New Mechanism - Provides new HOPE to the user that the claim will be delivered through
new technology developed into a sophisticated product
●​ Different way of delivering the same result
●​ "Not another supplement, but the first one that..."
●​ Technology angle, process angle, or ingredient angle
2. New Information - Makes users aware of problems that they were previously unaware of
and/or helps them better understand why previous mechanisms failed.
●​ Previously unknown "why" behind the problem
●​ Reframe the entire problem
●​ Reveal hidden obstacles others miss

3. New Identity - Builds relatability in sophisticated markets & creates buy-in for what the
product represents more than just the mechanism & information
●​ Redefine what using the product says about the customer
●​ "Not another [category], but for people who..."
●​ Cultural angle, tribal angle, or anti-establishment angle​

New Mechanism requires R&D investment in proprietary technology, patent protection or trade
secrets, technical communication capabilities, and demonstration/proof systems.

New Information needs subject matter expertise, content creation resources, educational
platform development, and long-term commitment to authority building.

New Identity demands brand strategy expertise, influencer relationship building, and authentic
storytelling capabilities

• 21d

[@Reda Mat](https://www.skool.com/@c8a1678b0657437984e6b3f8eb62e320?g=origins)

thanks 💪

Honestly, I applied the Spencer process.

After validating our product-market fit by using winning angles from competitors, I took the time to do deep research on Reddit. I identified as many sub-avatars as possible and followed the framework: 1 core emotion, 1 desire, and 1 behavior for each one. Then, I created 5 creatives for every angle.

My recommendation is that once your testing is validated (break-even or profitable), take the time to go deeper. List all your sub-avatars and angles, then test everything to gather as much data as possible.

Regarding product testing, I tested a lot of products using the copy-paste method, and nothing really worked for me. I only started getting results when I tested products with a new mechanism or a unique angle.

2[Reda Mat](https://www.skool.com/@reda-mat-1038?g=origins)

Or do you create your own ads while keeping the same core winning angle? Just curious, as it would help me with my next round of testing. Thanks!

Honestly, I applied the Spencer process.

After validating our product-market fit by using winning angles from competitors, I took the time to do deep research on Reddit. I identified as many sub-avatars as possible and followed the framework: 1 core emotion, 1 desire, and 1 behavior for each one. Then, I created 5 creatives for every angle.

My recommendation is that once your testing is validated (break-even or profitable), take the time to go deeper. List all your sub-avatars a... See more

title: "Rule Of 1 - 1 Desire, Avatar, Message - 👣 Evolve (Start Here)"

Rule Of 1 - 1 Desire, Avatar, Message

34:02

This is one of the most important videos to watch so you fully grasp why you need to go deep into the Desires, Avatars, Angles, and make it congruent to your LP.  
  
And also why your ad + LP should only appeal to 1 desire and 1 avatar to build more belief.  
  
This is certified Chad scaling sauce.  
  
Correction:  
**Angle = Emotional Trigger + \[Daily Struggle OR Ideal Outcome\]**  
  
Problem/Daily Struggle: Current pain + what makes it hell  
Aspirational/Ideal: Current emotion + dream state  
Your "meeting point" - where their feelings and your message connect  
  
If you were not selling a problem solving product for example, like fashion. You would then show what your avatar aspires to be or look like.  
  
The point is still the same. Per Schwartz's teachings, our goal is to be able to relate to our customers and enter their current emotional state through relating to their current state of emotions. And you can show that via a daily struggle or ideal outcome.
