FILE: What Women Want.txt
======================================================================

WHAT WOMEN WANT
Female Buyer Psychology for Ecom Broskis
by Lina Fahizul
Chief Ovary Officer​

​

WHAT’S ON THE YAPPING AGENDA:
The $31.8 Trillion Problem Nobody Talks About
Why this matters before you spend a dollar on ads. The stat that reframes everything.

Hunter vs. Gatherer: This Started 200,000 Years Ago
Men buy for status. Women buy for stability. The evolutionary psychology behind every funnel mistake you’re
making.

How She Actually Buys
The 8-stage female buying journey, why most funnels skip the first three stages, and the single most important
insight: women buy certainty, men buy potential.

The 6 Emotional Blockers
The framework I use auditing 8 and 9-figure brands. All six gates must open before she buys. One closed gate and
the sale dies, no matter how good the product is.

The 6 Ecom Bro Mistakes That Kill Female Conversions
Guilt, shame, aggressive CTAs, wrong proof stacks, and making her the problem. The playbook you’ve been handed
was designed for how men buy.

Creative Strategy: Applying This to Your Ads
How to research her properly, the blame check before you hit publish, and why UGC beats polished every time.
Plus: how female buyer psychology applies at TOF, MOF, and BOF with DTC examples across supplements, skincare,
haircare, activewear, and wellness.

​
The $31.8 Trillion Problem Nobody Talks About
Women control $31.8 trillion in global spending. They influence 85% of purchasing decisions. And 91% of
them feel misunderstood by the brands marketing to them.
You're building ecom brands. Most of your customers are women. And you've been handed a funnel
playbook that was designed by men, tested on men, and optimized for how men buy.
MEET THE CHIEF OVARY OFFICER

I've spoken to over 200 women. Interviewed them on why they buy, why they didn't buy, what
made them feel good, and what made them feel like shit. I've audited over 200 brands to find the
patterns. And I've used what I learned to help scale a single client's brand to $20 million in six
months.

200+ female buyers interviewed on why they buy (or don't)
200+ brand funnels audited to find what repels women
$20M revenue scaled for a single client in 6 months
Lina Fahizul Chief Ovary Officer · Direct Response & Female Buyer Psychology
I specialise in direct response copy and female buyer psychology. I help brand owners (primarily men
selling to women) understand how to sell in a way that makes you feel good as a marketer and makes
the woman on the other end of the ad feel good too. The same patterns show up in every audit. And
that's actually great news for everyone in this room, because it means the fix is learnable.
Before we get into the framework I use when I audit 8 and 9-figure brands, I want to go back. Way back.

Hunter vs. Gatherer: This Started 200,000 Years Ago

You've heard of 'hunter vs. gatherer.' Most people think this is a fun anthropology fact and move on. But
this is actually the foundation of everything I'm going to teach you today.
Men hunted. The job was to identify a target, move fast, secure the kill. Linear. Decisive. High-risk,
high-reward. Status came from being the one who brought something home.
Women gathered. The job was to navigate the environment carefully, assess what was safe to pick, plan
for sustainability, and bring back enough for the community. Longer process. Risk-averse. Oriented
toward stability and security, not status.

Fast forward 200,000 years. The buying behavior hasn't changed.

Men buy for STATUS. The watch, the car, the new software tool, the premium course. 'This is who I am
and who I'm becoming.' Status-acquisition. It's fast, it's decisive, it responds to competition and scarcity.
Women buy for STABILITY. 'Is this safe? Will this work? What happens if it doesn't? What does my life
look like with this, and without it? Who else has done this?' Security-seeking. It's thorough, it's cyclical,
and it responds to certainty, not urgency.
This isn't a stereotype. This is literally a different stress response in the brain. Under purchasing pressure,
a man's fight instinct activates: he moves toward action. A woman's tend-and-befriend instinct activates:
she moves toward safety. Toward community. Away from anything that feels like a threat.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR YOUR FUNNEL

❌ Countdown timer: What NOT to say:
"OFFER ENDS IN 00:04:32. BUY NOW"
✅ What actually works:
"I only work with 20 brands at a time so I can actually move the needle. There are 3 spots left this quarter."
❌ "ONLY 3 LEFT" banner: What NOT to say:
"ONLY 3 LEFT. ACT FAST OR MISS OUT FOREVER"
✅ What actually works:
"Because I review every order personally, I cap intake at 50 units per week. We're at 47."
❌ "Are you SICK AND TIRED" opener: What NOT to say:
"Are you SICK AND TIRED of your skin not clearing up?!"
✅ What actually works:
"If you've tried everything and nothing has worked, there's a reason, and it's not you."

She's not bouncing because she's indecisive. She's bouncing because your funnel told her nervous system
it wasn't safe to stay.

How She Actually Buys
Men buy linearly. Problem. Solution. Purchase. Six minutes on average.
​
Women buy in stages. And these stages are emotional before they're ever logical.​
The mistake most of ecom founders make is jumping straight to the logical sell. ​
​
'Here's the product, here are the ingredients, here's the clinical study, here's the price.' ​
​
That's stage four. She hasn't cleared stages one, two, and three yet. You're asking her to buy before she
feels safe enough to consider it.

Think of it like dating. You don't walk up to someone on a first date and say 'here's my
five-year financial plan, here's my dental record, would you like to commit?' That's not how
relationships work. You build rapport first. You earn the right to the ask.

HER 8-STAGE BUYING JOURNEY

Stage 1: Emotional Resonance: 'I feel seen. This person understands my experience.'
Stage 2: Emotional Safety: 'I feel safe considering this. This doesn't feel manipulative.'
Stage 3: Emotional Permission: 'I feel allowed to want this. I'm not being greedy or unrealistic.'
Stage 4: Logical Validation: 'Okay, now let me evaluate if this actually makes sense.'
Stage 5: Emotional Confirmation: 'Does this still feel right? Let me check in with my gut.'
Stage 6: Social Validation: 'What would people like me think? Do others like me do this?'
Stage 7: Final Emotional Check: 'One more gut check before I commit.'
Stage 8: Purchase.

Most male-designed funnels jump straight to Stage 4 and wonder why women don't convert.

THE SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT INSIGHT

Women buy CERTAINTY. Men buy POTENTIAL.

'Scale to $100K months!' hits men.
'Here's the exact process, what to expect each week, what happens when you finish, and what we do if it
doesn't work' hits women.
Vagueness kills female conversions. Gaps in information get filled with worst-case scenarios. Her brain is
hardwired for risk assessment. Every unanswered question becomes an assumption. Every assumption
skews negative.

The 6 Emotional Blockers from My Female Buying
Framework
This is the framework I use when I audit brands. I have never done an audit (at any level, 8-figure,
9-figure) where at least four of these six weren't being triggered. Not once.

Blocker 1: 'Do I feel understood?'
Generic pain points don't open this gate. 'Tired of struggling with your skin?' doesn't cut it. You have to
describe her experience so precisely she thinks you've been reading her diary. The difference between
'feeling stuck' and actually painting the 3am moment where she's lying awake wondering if she's the
only one who can't figure this out. One is vague. One is a mirror.

❌ Weak: What NOT to say:
"Tired of feeling stuck in your business?"
✅ What actually works:

"You're posting every day, watching others hit milestones you've been working toward for months, and quietly
wondering if something is fundamentally wrong with you. It's not. It's your funnel."

Blocker 2: 'Do I feel safe trusting this?'
Women are behavior-watchers, not word-takers. She's not reading your testimonials thinking 'great,
social proof.' She's watching how you talk about other customers. She's noticing if your page feels
cluttered. She's clocking whether your copy respects her intelligence or talks down to her. Trust is built
through consistency of signal, not through declarations. You can't just say 'we care.' She has to feel it in
every line.

Blocker 3: 'Can I see predictable results?'
She doesn't want a highlight reel of your best case scenarios. She wants to know what happens in the
average case. What does the journey look like? How long does it take? What should she realistically
expect? Timelines aren't just useful. They're security infrastructure. They let her plan, budget her energy,
and calculate whether success is actually possible for her.

❌ Vague: What NOT to say:
"Transform your skin in just weeks!"
✅ What actually works:

"Most customers see initial improvement within 14 days. Full results develop over 60-90 days as the formula
works through your skin cycle. Here's what to expect week by week."

Blocker 4: 'Can I picture my life after this?'
Not an aspirational vision board. A specific, emotionally vivid picture of her actual day-to-day life once
your product has done its job. Not 'imagine feeling confident!' She wants: what does Tuesday morning
feel like? What does she notice is different? What's the first thing someone says to her? This is the vision
work most brands completely skip.

Blocker 5: 'Do I feel supported?'
Women are community buyers. A single founder-as-expert sell doesn't land as well as 'here's the team,
here's what happens if you have a problem, here's how to reach us.' Open channels of communication
equal trust. If you close those channels (unanswered DMs, generic autoresponders, buried contact
pages), she notices. Every time.

Blocker 6: 'Is this who I am?'
She's not just buying a product. She's deciding if buying this product is consistent with her identity. You
cannot ask a woman to become someone new. You have to show her that buying your product is an
expression of who she already is, just more fully.

❌ Identity trap: What NOT to say:
"Become the woman who finally has it all together."
✅ What actually works:

"For the woman who already knows what she wants. This is how she gets there faster."

The 6 Ecom Bro Mistakes That Kill Female Conversions
Mistake 1: Selling outcomes before earning emotional trust
The typical ecom ad goes straight to product features and benefits. 'Clinically proven. 30% more
effective. 60-day guarantee.' She doesn't care yet. You haven't earned the right to logic. Lead with her
emotional state first. Name what she's feeling before you tell her what you sell.

Mistake 2: Aggressive CTAs
'BUY NOW. CLAIM YOUR SPOT. ACT FAST OR MISS OUT.' Every one of these registers as a threat to the
female nervous system. It's not motivation. It's pressure. And women don't buy under pressure. They
retreat. Switch your language entirely.

❌ Aggressive CTA: What NOT to say:
"BUY NOW. ONLY 3 LEFT, DON'T MISS OUT"
✅ What actually works:

"Why not try this for 30 days? If it doesn't work, we'll make it right."

Mistake 3: The wrong proof stack
Revenue screenshots. Dramatic before/after photos. 'I made $47K in 3 days.' That's male proof. It
activates status-seeking, not security-seeking. Female proof looks completely different.
Male proof: 'I made $47K in 3 days'Female proof: 'Before, I felt like I was doing everything
wrong and nothing was working. After, I finally understood the system. I wasn't guessing
anymore. I feel capable again.'
Emotional transformation. Milestone stories. Proof from people she can see herself in: different ages,
different life stages, different starting points.

Mistake 4: Treating 'women' as a target audience
If your creative brief says 'target audience: women,' your marketing is going to be generic at best and
condescending at worst. 'Women' is not a psychographic. She's 24 buying her first skincare routine. She's
38 and a working mum with four minutes to make a decision. She's 51 and done being talked to like she's
invisible. Stop writing for 'women.' Start writing for her, specifically.

Mistake 5: Forgetting she's watching everything
Women notice incongruences. Not as thoughts. As feelings. You say 'we care about our customers' on
your homepage and then she can't find a customer service email. That's a feeling of doubt she can't
articulate. She just knows something feels off. And she leaves. Your funnel is every touchpoint. Every ad.
Every email. Every reply to a comment. She's building a case for whether to trust you.

Mistake 6: Guilt, shame, and making her the problem
This is the one that makes me want to flip tables every time I see it in an audit.
'Are you SICK AND TIRED of failing at [X]?' 'Still struggling with [Y]?' 'Why isn't [Z] working for you?'
Pain-point agitation is in every funnel course ever created. Make her feel the problem, then offer the
solution. It works beautifully on men. Men externalize failure. They hear 'you're losing the game' and
they get competitive.
Women internalize failure. When you open your ad with 'are you still struggling,' what she hears is: yes,
you're the problem. You've been failing. You should feel bad about this. And then she leaves. Not
because she doesn't want your solution. Because nobody buys from someone who just made them feel
like garbage.

The rule: the blame is always external. Always.It's not her fault her skin isn't clearing up. It's
that nobody told her about the hormonal connection to breakouts until now. It's not her fault
her business isn't growing. It's that every template she was handed was designed for how men
buy. It's not her fault the supplements aren't working. It's that the formulas she's been sold
were tested on men.Remove her from the dock entirely. She is not the defendant in your copy.

❌ Blame internal: What NOT to say:
"Are you SICK AND TIRED of your skin failing you?"
✅ What actually works:

"Your skin isn't the problem. The routine you were sold is. Here's what actually works for the hormonal
breakout cycle nobody talks about."

Creative Strategy: Applying This to Your Ads
This is where people get it conceptually and fall down in execution. So let's make it practical.

How to research her (without guessing)
Stop asking your male team what women want. The number of audits I've done where the only research
conducted was 'we asked our three male co-founders and they thought the copy sounded fine' is
genuinely alarming.
Go where she talks to herself, not to you.
YOUR RESEARCH SOURCES

●​ Reddit threads in your niche: she's brutally honest when she thinks no one's selling to her
●​ Amazon reviews for your product category: mine 1-star AND 5-star reviews, both tell you what
she needs to feel
●​ Comment sections on competitors' ads: she'll tell you exactly what's missing
●​ TikTok comments on products she already uses: look for 'I wish this had...' and 'finally something
that...'
●​ Her own words in customer emails and support tickets: your CS inbox is a goldmine
She is telling you exactly what she needs to hear. She's describing her fears, her frustrations, her failed
attempts, what she wished someone had told her. Mine that language. Use her words back to her almost
verbatim. That's not manipulation. That's actually listening.

What your ads should actually be doing
The ads that convert female audiences are selling the feeling she gets before she even owns the product.
The safety of knowing this exists. The relief of finding something that gets it. The permission to want this
for herself.
FEMALE-OPTIMIZED AD STRUCTURE

Lead with her emotional state right now, before she's found you
Name what she's tried that hasn't worked (without blaming her for trying it)
Introduce the reason nothing worked (external cause, not her failure)
Show her someone like her who crossed that bridge
One clear, low-pressure next step

The blame check: before you hit publish
Read your first two lines out loud. Ask yourself: is the subject of this sentence my customer, or is it the
problem she's dealing with?
Women buy from brands that make them feel seen and exonerated, not from brands that remind them
of everything they haven't figured out yet.
Every line that positions her as the one who got it wrong closes an emotional gate. Disassociate her from
the failure. Every time. Without exception.

TOF / MOF / BOF: Female Psychology at Every Funnel Stage
TOF: Top of Funnel (She Doesn't Know You Yet)
Your job at TOF is not to sell. It’s not even to convert. Your job is to make her feel understood by a
stranger.
Women decide within seconds whether a brand is worth paying attention to, and that decision is entirely
emotional. She’s not asking “is this product good?” She’s asking “does this brand get me?” That answer
lives in your hook. Your first frame. Your first line of copy.
The biggest TOF mistake: leading with the product. She doesn’t care about your product yet. She cares
about her life. Lead with her emotional state. Name the exact feeling she’s sitting in right now, before
she’s found you. And remember: at TOF, the blame is always external. Never open a cold ad by making
her feel like she’s the problem.
WHAT WORKS AT TOF:

•​ Mirror her current emotional reality, not her aspirational one
•​ Externalise the problem completely (it’s not her fault)
•​ Soft curiosity gap over hard sell
•​ No aggressive CTAs — “learn more” outperforms “shop now” at this stage
EXAMPLES:

❌
✅

Supplements (weight loss):
Tired of struggling with your weight? Our formula targets stubborn fat.
If you’ve been eating well and exercising for months and the scale still won’t move, it’s not your effort. It’s
your cortisol. And nobody told you that.

MOF: Middle of Funnel (She Knows You, She's Not Sure Yet)
MOF is where most brands drop the ball. They’ve got her attention. She’s been following along. And then
they pivot straight into sales mode: stacked testimonials, price anchoring, countdown timers. She feels
the switch. And it makes her distrust you.
At MOF your job is to build the case for certainty. Not hype. Certainty. She needs to see proof that this
works for someone who looks like her, sounds like her, started where she started. She needs to
understand the process, not just the outcome. And she needs to feel like you’ve thought about what
happens if it doesn’t work, not just when it does.
The Trust Loop matters most here. She needs multiple touchpoints, and each one needs to reinforce the
same message. Inconsistency between your ads, your email, and your landing page doesn’t just confuse
her. It registers as a red flag.
WHAT WORKS AT MOF:

•​ Single transformation stories, not stacked screenshots
•​ Process explainers: what happens week by week, what to expect
•​ Objection handling that takes her concerns seriously
•​ “People like me” proof: variety of ages, life stages, starting points
•​ Founder visibility and open communication signals

❌
✅

Haircare (thinning hair):
Join 50,000 women who’ve transformed their hair.
“Here’s what the first eight weeks look like, specifically if you’re postpartum, if your hair is fine and
colour-treated, and if you’ve already tried two other products that didn’t work. We built this for that exact
situation because that’s who most of our customers are when they start.”

BOF: Bottom of Funnel (She's Ready, She Just Needs Permission)
She’s done the research. She knows the product. She’s been following you for weeks. And she still hasn’t
bought.
She’s not stuck on price. She’s not stuck on logistics. She’s waiting for permission.
Permission to invest in herself. Permission to spend the money. Permission to believe this will work for
her specifically, not just for the people in the testimonials. She’s calculated the risk. She’s sitting at a door
that’s almost open, waiting for someone to tell her it’s okay to walk through.
Your BOF creative has one job: be the permission-giver, not the pressure-applier.
Everything you’ve been taught about closing (urgency, scarcity, fear of missing out, countdown timers) is
not permission. It’s pressure. And pressure at BOF doesn’t close female buyers. It breaks the trust you’ve
spent weeks building and sends her back to the start of the loop.
WHAT WORKS AT BOF:

•​ Inviting CTAs: “Why not give yourself this?” / “Ready when you are”
•​ Guarantees framed around her confidence, not your product
•​ FAQ that answers the things she’s afraid to ask
•​ Clear description of exactly what happens after she clicks
•​ No countdown timers unless the scarcity is real and fully explained

The throughline across all three: TOF earns her attention by making her feel understood. MOF builds her
certainty by showing her the process and the proof. BOF gives her permission by removing pressure and
completing the picture. Each stage has a different emotional job. The psychology is the same at every
level.

The Two Things That Beat Every Tactic

Before I send you off with a cheat sheet and a to-do list, I want to give you the thing that sits underneath
all of it. Because you could memorize every blocker, every mistake, every ad formula I've shared today,
and still get it wrong if you don't have these two things.

Empathy. And curiosity.That's it. Those two qualities, genuinely practiced, will do more for
your conversions with female buyers than any tactic you'll ever learn.

Empathy is not sympathy.
Sympathy is 'I feel bad for you.' That's not what she needs, and honestly it's a little condescending when
it shows up in copy.
Empathy is 'I have taken the time to understand your world so thoroughly that when you read my copy,
you feel less alone in it.' That's a completely different thing. Sympathy is passive. Empathy is work.
Real empathy in marketing means you've done the research. You know what she's tried before you
showed up. You know why it didn't work, and more importantly, you know how it made her feel when it
didn't work. You know the internal story she tells herself about why she's still dealing with this problem.
You know what she's afraid people will think if she tries something new and it fails again.
When you know all of that, you don't have to manufacture connection. It's just there. Because she reads
your copy and thinks 'how do they know that?' And that feeling, that split second of being genuinely
seen, is what opens Emotional Blocker 1. Everything else flows from it.

The brands that get this right don't sound like they're marketing. They sound like they've been
paying attention.

Curiosity is the other half, and most people completely skip it.
Curiosity means you are genuinely interested in the gap between what she tells you and what she
means.
Women are symbolic communicators. When she says 'I just want something that works,' she's not giving
you a product requirement. She's telling you she's exhausted from being let down. When she says 'I
don't have time,' she's not telling you her schedule. She's telling you she doesn't have energy left to
invest in something that might not pay off.
Curiosity means you don't take the surface answer and run with it. You sit with it. You keep asking, in
your research, in your copy, in the way you structure your offer: 'what does she actually mean by that?'
Because the distance between what she says and what she means is exactly where most marketing
breaks down.
Curiosity also changes how you write. When you're genuinely curious about your customer, your copy
stops sounding like a pitch and starts sounding like a conversation. You ask better questions in your
hooks. You make observations instead of declarations. You leave space for her to arrive at her own
conclusion instead of dragging her to yours.
And this matters enormously for female buyers specifically, because women don't want to be led to your
conclusion. They want to be led to their own. The role of your marketing is to create the conditions
where her conclusion happens to be 'yes.' That's a fundamentally different posture than most ecom
founders take.

The One Thing to Take Into Your Next Creative Brief
Before your next ad. Your next product page. Your next email. Ask yourself one question:

Does this make her feel understood, or does it make her feel like she's
failing?

🙂

If the first three sentences of anything you write lead with her failure state without immediately creating
a path to safety, go back to the drawing board ​

​
​
​

THE END

The Cheat Sheet

THE 6 EMOTIONAL BLOCKERS: ALL MUST OPEN BEFORE SHE BUYS

1. Do I feel understood?
2. Do I feel safe trusting this?
3. Can I see predictable results?
4. Can I picture my life after this?
5. Do I feel supported?
6. Is this who I am?

MEN VS. WOMEN: BUYING AT A GLANCE

Men: Status-driven, linear, logic-first, respond to scarcity and competition
Women: Stability-driven, cyclical, emotion-first, respond to certainty and community
Men buy POTENTIAL. Women buy CERTAINTY
Men externalize failure. Women internalize it (never blame her)

THE 6 MISTAKES TO AVOID FROM DAY ONE

1. Leading with logic before earning emotional trust
2. Aggressive CTAs ('BUY NOW', 'ACT FAST', 'DON'T MISS OUT')
3. Wrong proof stack (revenue screenshots vs. emotional transformation stories)
4. Treating 'women' as a target audience instead of a specific person
5. Forgetting she's watching every touchpoint for inconsistency
6. Making her the problem (blame is always external, always)

FEMALE-OPTIMIZED AD FORMULA

Lead with her emotional state NOW
Name what she's tried (without blaming her for trying)
Introduce the real reason it didn't work (external cause)
Show someone like her who made it through
One clear, low-pressure CTA

WHERE TO RESEARCH HER

Reddit threads in your niche
Amazon 1-star and 5-star reviews in your category
Competitor ad comment sections
TikTok comments on products she already uses
Your own CS inbox and customer emails
